proposals.title: Covalent Inhibition of NSUN2 as a Synthetic Lethal Strategy for Mutant p53-High Cancers
proposals.motivation: Mutant p53 proteins, often stabilized at high abundance, are common oncogenic drivers yet remain largely undrugged. Recent work, including from our lab, identifies high mutant p53 protein levels as a potential therapeutic window. Simultaneously, RNA methyltransferases like NSUN2 are emerging as critical regulators of cancer cell survival, but their therapeutic targeting is underexplored. This proposal seeks to bridge these areas by testing whether sustained, covalent inhibition of NSUN2 creates a unique vulnerability in mutant p53-high cancers.
proposals.hypothesis: Covalent, but not reversible, inhibition of the RNA methyltransferase NSUN2 will induce a distinct and persistent RNA methylation landscape that is synthetically lethal in cells with high mutant p53 protein abundance, providing a genotype-selective therapeutic strategy.
proposals.method: We will employ a chemical genomics screen using isogenic cell line pairs (e.g., TP53-null vs. TP53-mutant) engineered for high mutant p53 expression. These lines will be treated with matched covalent (e.g., acrylamide-bearing) and reversible inhibitors of NSUN2, developed via structure-guided design from known NSUN2 binders. Cell viability will be measured via high-content imaging. The functional necessity of covalent engagement will be assessed by comparing phenotypic outcomes and by profiling the global RNA methylation landscape (m5C) via bisulfite sequencing after transient versus sustained inhibitor washout.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a direct causal link between sustained target engagement (via covalent inhibition) and a genotype-selective phenotypic outcome, moving beyond binding affinity to functional selectivity. It will validate or refute NSUN2 as a synthetic lethal target in mutant p53 contexts and provide a chemical probe pair to dissect the role of RNA methylation dynamics in oncogene addiction.
proposals.novelty: While synthetic lethality with mutant p53 is sought after, targeting RNA methylation enzymes in this context is novel. The explicit comparison of covalent versus reversible inhibitors of the same target to achieve therapeutic-window selectivity, rather than just binding selectivity, is a distinct and underexplored application of covalent chemical probes.

proposals.title: Fragment-Based Discovery of Cryptic Allosteric Pockets in the RNA Methyltransferase NSUN2
proposals.motivation: NSUN2 is an oncogenic RNA methyltransferase implicated in various cancers, but its therapeutic targeting has been limited. Current strategies focus on covalent inhibition of its catalytic cysteine, which presents selectivity challenges and may be insufficient for durable therapeutic effects. Identifying alternative, allosteric binding sites could unlock novel, more selective chemical modalities to modulate NSUN2 function, analogous to the paradigm shift seen with KRAS(G12C) inhibitors.
proposals.hypothesis: NSUN2 possesses latent, cryptic pockets that can be stabilized and revealed by small-molecule fragments, providing novel allosteric sites for inhibitor development distinct from the catalytic active site.
proposals.method: We will employ a fragment-based lead discovery (FBLD) platform using a diverse, rule-of-three compliant library screened against recombinant NSUN2 via biophysical methods (SPR, DSF). Fragment hits will be advanced into co-crystallization trials with NSUN2 using high-throughput X-ray crystallography. For complexes recalcitrant to crystallization, cryo-EM will be employed. Computational analysis of fragment-bound structures will guide iterative chemical elaboration using synthetic chemistry, informed by principles of proteolysis-targeting chimeras (PROTACs) and molecular glues to assess potential for induced proximity mechanisms.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first structural map of ligandable, non-catalytic sites on NSUN2, providing novel chemical starting points for allosteric inhibitor development. It will establish a generalizable framework for targeting RNA-modifying enzymes considered 'undruggable' via traditional active-site approaches, expanding the chemical biology toolkit for epigenetic regulation.
proposals.novelty: This directly challenges the singular focus on NSUN2's catalytic cysteine by applying an allosteric screening paradigm proven in other hard-to-drug targets. The systematic integration of FBLD with high-throughput structural biology to probe NSUN2's conformational landscape for cryptic pockets is unprecedented.

proposals.title: A Chemically Tunable Dimerization Platform to Empirically Define the Therapeutic Vector for NSUN2-tRNA Interaction Modulation in Disease
proposals.motivation: The RNA methyltransferase NSUN2 modifies tRNAs, influencing translation and cellular stress responses, and is implicated in cancer and neurodevelopmental disorders. However, it is unknown whether therapeutic benefit requires stabilizing or inhibiting its interaction with tRNA substrates, creating a fundamental barrier to rational drug discovery. This critical ambiguity prevents the targeted development of small-molecule glues or degraders for NSUN2-related pathologies.
proposals.hypothesis: The phenotypic consequences of modulating the NSUN2-tRNA interaction are not inherently therapeutic in one direction; instead, the optimal therapeutic vector (stabilization vs. inhibition) is context-dependent and can be determined empirically using a tunable cellular system.
proposals.method: We will engineer a cellular system where NSUN2 is fused to one half of a chemically induced dimerization (CID) domain (e.g., FRB) and a core tRNA-binding domain or specific tRNA is fused to the complementary domain (FKBP). Using a panel of rapamycin analogs and synthetic dimerizers with varying affinities and pharmacokinetic properties, we will titrate interaction strength from sub-physiological to hyper-stabilized states. This tunable system will be deployed in isogenic disease models (e.g., TP53-mutant cancer lines, neuronal progenitors with NSUN2 mutations) for high-content imaging screens, quantifying hallmarks like proliferation, apoptosis, and differentiation to generate a functional map of interaction modulation.
proposals.contribution: This work will resolve the fundamental ambiguity surrounding NSUN2 targeting, providing a definitive, data-driven therapeutic hypothesis for specific disease contexts. It will establish a generalizable CID-based platform for empirically determining the therapeutic direction of protein-RNA interactions, bridging chemical biology and phenotypic screening.
proposals.novelty: While CID systems are used to control protein-protein interactions, their application to titrate and functionally decode a therapeutically ambiguous protein-RNA interaction is novel. This approach inverts the standard paradigm by using chemistry to first define the therapeutic hypothesis before compound discovery.

proposals.title: Mapping the Epitranscriptomic Network Collapse: A Systems Biology Approach to tRNA m5C-Dependent Translation Vulnerabilities in Cancer
proposals.motivation: The tRNA methyltransferase NSUN2 is frequently overexpressed in cancers, and its loss leads to broad but poorly understood phenotypic consequences, including differentiation defects and metabolic shifts. Current studies are correlative, failing to mechanistically link the loss of a single RNA modification to the cascade of cellular failures. A systems-level understanding of how m5C loss destabilizes the translation network is needed to identify key vulnerabilities for therapeutic targeting.
proposals.hypothesis: NSUN2 acts as a keystone node in an epitranscriptomic network, and its inhibition triggers a predictable cascade of translation failures, where specific mRNA substrates with particular codon usage patterns are most vulnerable, leading to downstream network collapse and phenotypic outcomes.
proposals.method: We will use a chemical biology approach, employing a potent and selective NSUN2 inhibitor (e.g., a small-molecule glue or PROTAC derived from pre-2026 tool compounds) to acutely deplete tRNA m5C in isogenic cancer cell lines. We will then apply Ribo-seq to measure ribosome occupancy and translation efficiency, coupled with pulsed SILAC quantitative proteomics to track proteomic dynamics. These multi-omics datasets will be integrated using a network perturbation model, adapting algorithms from ecological cascading failure analysis (e.g., Influence Maximization or threshold models) to predict and validate the most fragile nodes in the translation network.
proposals.contribution: This work will move the field from phenotype correlation to a predictive, mechanistic model of epitranscriptomic regulation. It will define a 'vulnerability map' of translation dependent on tRNA m5C, identifying specific proteins and pathways whose synthesis is most sensitive to this modification. This provides a framework for understanding how targeting a single RNA writer can have pleiotropic effects and may reveal novel synthetic lethal interactions for cancer therapy.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in applying ecological network collapse theory to epitranscriptomics and in combining acute chemical inhibition with multi-omics and computational network modeling to move beyond static mapping to dynamic, causal understanding.

proposals.title: Targeted Degradation of NSUN2 to Probe tRNA m5C-Dependent Translation Stress as a Synthetic Lethality with Mutant p53
proposals.motivation: The tRNA methyltransferase NSUN2 installs m5C modifications critical for tRNA stability and efficient translation. Pre-2026 work shows NSUN2 is upregulated in many cancers and its loss can impair translation of specific oncogenic transcripts. Concurrently, mutant p53 proteins, which are common in cancer, often create a unique cellular dependency on stress-response pathways, but direct, specific links to the tRNA epitranscriptome remain unexplored.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that PROTAC-mediated degradation of NSUN2 will induce a specific translation stress state via loss of tRNA m5C, and that cancer cells harboring mutant p53 will be uniquely vulnerable to this perturbation, revealing a novel synthetic lethal interaction.
proposals.method: We will design and synthesize heterobifunctional PROTACs linking a known NSUN2-targeting small molecule (e.g., from pre-2026 inhibitor scaffolds) to ligands for the VHL or CRBN E3 ubiquitin ligase. Using isogenic cancer cell lines differing only in TP53 status, we will quantify NSUN2 degradation, tRNA m5C loss via bisulfite-seq, and phenotypic consequences. Ribosome profiling and RNA-seq will identify transcripts whose translation is specifically disrupted upon NSUN2 loss, correlating with mutant p53-specific cell death.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a first-in-class chemical degrader for a tRNA methyltransferase, providing a precise tool to study the functional output of the tRNA epitranscriptome. It will define a novel vulnerability in mutant p53 cancers, potentially identifying a new therapeutic strategy that combines epitranscriptomic perturbation with a common oncogenic context.
proposals.novelty: While PROTACs have targeted kinases and transcription factors, applying them to a tRNA-modifying enzyme to study translation-level synthetic lethality is unexplored. The proposal uniquely fuses Schreiber's expertise in chemical inducers of proximity with the emerging biology of tRNA modifications and mutant p53 dependency.

proposals.title: Targeting tRNA Methylation: A Chemical Biology Approach to Elucidate the Phenotypic Consequences of NSUN2 Inhibition in Cancer
proposals.motivation: RNA modifications, particularly 5-methylcytosine (m5C) on tRNAs catalyzed by NSUN2, are emerging as critical regulators of translation and cellular stress responses, with NSUN2 frequently overexpressed in cancers. However, the specific downstream proteomic and phenotypic consequences of its inhibition remain poorly mapped, representing a significant gap in understanding this potential therapeutic vulnerability. This project aims to bridge chemical biology with functional genomics to define the actionable biology triggered by loss of this tRNA modification.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that pharmacological inhibition of NSUN2 will disrupt the translation of a specific subset of proteins critical for cancer cell proliferation and survival, and that the dependency on this pathway will vary across cancer lineages based on their underlying oncogenic drivers and stress states.
proposals.method: We will employ a multi-faceted chemical biology strategy. First, we will use targeted protein degradation (PROTACs) or existing small-molecule probes to acutely inhibit NSUN2 function in a panel of cancer cell lines. Downstream consequences will be assessed via ribosome profiling and quantitative mass spectrometry to identify changes in translation efficiency and protein abundance. Phenotypic outputs (proliferation, apoptosis, stress granule formation) will be correlated with proteomic shifts using multivariate analysis. Finally, we will apply genetic dependency maps (e.g., CRISPR screens) to identify synthetic lethal interactions with NSUN2 loss.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a direct, causal link between NSUN2-mediated tRNA methylation, the translation of specific protein networks, and cancer cell fitness. It will identify candidate biomarkers for sensitivity to NSUN2 inhibition and reveal novel combinatorial therapeutic strategies. The project will provide a framework for studying RNA-modifying enzymes as druggable nodes in cancer.
proposals.novelty: While NSUN2's role in tRNA methylation is known, this proposal uniquely applies the Schreiber lab's expertise in chemical probes and phenotypic screening to systematically dissect the *consequences* of its inhibition at the protein and functional level, moving beyond correlative studies to establish mechanism-based therapeutic hypotheses.

proposals.title: Fragment-Based Discovery of Selective Covalent Probes for NSUN RNA Methyltransferases via Cysteine-Directed ABPP
proposals.motivation: The NSUN family of RNA methyltransferases are critical regulators of gene expression and cellular metabolism, with specific members like NSUN2 and NSUN3 linked to developmental disorders and cancer. However, the functional dissection of individual NSUN proteins, particularly NSUN1, NSUN3, NSUN4, NSUN5, and NSUN7, is severely hampered by a lack of selective chemical probes. Current cysteine-reactive activity-based protein profiling (ABPP) libraries, while powerful, often yield broad-spectrum hits that cannot discriminate between these closely related enzymes with conserved catalytic cysteines.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a fragment-based ABPP approach, using minimal electrophilic scaffolds, will identify weak, non-selective covalent binders to the conserved active-site cysteine across NSUN proteins, and that subsequent structure-guided elaboration of these fragments can confer high selectivity for individual family members.
proposals.method: We will employ a cysteine-directed ABPP platform using a focused library of low-molecular-weight (<200 Da) electrophilic fragments (e.g., acrylamides, chloroacetamides) bearing alkyne handles. This library will be screened against a panel of recombinant NSUN proteins (NSUN1, 3, 4, 5, 7) via competitive gel-based or mass spectrometry-based ABPP. Initial fragment hits will be co-crystallized with target NSUNs to obtain high-resolution structures. Using these structures, iterative chemical synthesis will be performed to elaborate the fragment core, guided by computational docking (e.g., Schrödinger Suite) to exploit unique subpocket residues adjacent to the target cysteine in each NSUN protein, thereby engineering selectivity.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first suite of potent and selective covalent chemical probes for understudied NSUN methyltransferases, enabling precise functional studies in cells. It will establish a generalizable fragment-based ABPP methodology for achieving selectivity within challenging enzyme families, moving beyond broad-reactive screens. The resulting probes will serve as valuable tools for chemical biology and as potential starting points for therapeutic development.
proposals.novelty: While FBDD is established for non-covalent kinase inhibitors, its adaptation to covalent, cysteine-targeting ABPP for RNA-modifying enzymes is novel. The strategy explicitly addresses the selectivity paradox in covalent probe discovery by borrowing and adapting the 'fragment elaboration' paradigm from a different, but analogously challenging, protein family.

proposals.title: Targeted Degradation of the RNA Methyltransferase NSUN2 Using Bifunctional PROTACs and Molecular Glues
proposals.motivation: NSUN2 is an RNA methyltransferase that catalyzes m5C modification on tRNAs and mRNAs, with established roles in promoting oncogenesis, stem cell maintenance, and viral replication. Pre-2026 work shows that genetic depletion of NSUN2 has profound anti-proliferative effects in cancer models, but existing small-molecule inhibitors lack sufficient potency and specificity for robust therapeutic interrogation. Targeted protein degradation offers a superior pharmacological strategy to achieve a sustained, loss-of-function phenotype akin to genetic knockout.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that recruiting NSUN2 to an E3 ubiquitin ligase via a bifunctional PROTAC or a molecular glue will induce its efficient and selective ubiquitination and proteasomal degradation, leading to more potent and durable biological effects than catalytic inhibition alone.
proposals.method: We will leverage Schreiber lab expertise in molecular glues and bifunctional compound design. First, we will screen existing NSUN2-binding fragments and known inhibitors (e.g., Sinefungin analogs) for their ability to form ternary complexes with NSUN2 and E3 ligases (e.g., CRBN, VHL) using cellular thermal shift assays and NanoBRET-based proximity assays. High-throughput phenotypic screening of diverse compound libraries will be conducted in parallel to identify novel molecular glue degraders. Lead compounds will be optimized via structure-activity relationship studies guided by co-crystal structures of NSUN2-ligand complexes and ternary complex modeling, with degradation efficacy validated by immunoblotting, cycloheximide chase assays, and RNA m5C-seq.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first pharmacological degraders of NSUN2, providing a critical chemical tool to dissect the non-catalytic and scaffolding functions of NSUN2 in RNA biology and disease. It will establish a proof-of-concept that RNA-modifying enzymes are viable targets for induced proximity degradation, expanding the therapeutic modality beyond conventional inhibition.
proposals.novelty: While PROTACs have been developed for kinases and transcription factors, their application to RNA methyltransferases remains unexplored. This project uniquely combines Schreiber's pioneering work in molecular glues and bifunctional degraders with the emerging biology of epitranscriptomic targets, moving beyond inhibition to complete protein elimination.

proposals.title: Defining Small-Molecule Pharmacophores for Direct E3 Ligase Engagement
proposals.motivation: The therapeutic paradigm of targeted protein degradation (TPD) relies on heterobifunctional molecules that recruit E3 ubiquitin ligases to neo-substrates. However, the field operates under the assumption that E3 ligase recruitment is exclusively mediated by recognition of specific peptide motifs or structural domains on native adapter proteins. This peptide-centric view limits the rational design of molecular glues and degraders, as it fails to explore whether E3 ligases possess intrinsic, druggable small-molecule binding pockets that could be exploited for more direct and potent engagement.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that certain E3 ligases contain cryptic, allosteric small-molecule binding sites distinct from their native protein-protein interaction interfaces, and that these sites can be engaged by specific, definable pharmacophores to modulate ligase activity or substrate specificity.
proposals.method: We will employ a multi-pronged approach: 1) Using Schreiber's diversity-oriented synthesis (DOS) libraries and phenotypic screening in engineered cell lines reporting on specific E3 activities, we will identify small-molecule 'monovalent' binders of E3 ligases like VHL or CRBN. 2) For validated hits, we will determine co-crystal structures to define atomic-level binding modes. 3) Leveraging these structural data, we will use computational algorithms (e.g., Schrödinger's Phase Shape Screening, MOE's pharmacophore elucidation tools) to generate 3D pharmacophore models. 4) These models will guide the synthesis of focused libraries to refine structure-activity relationships (SAR) and validate the pharmacophore.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic evidence and structural definition of small-molecule pharmacophores for direct E3 ligase binding. It will establish a new conceptual framework for E3 engagement beyond peptide mimicry, directly enabling the design of next-generation molecular glues with improved selectivity and physicochemical properties. The identified pharmacophores will serve as foundational templates for future TPD campaigns.
proposals.novelty: While others have discovered molecular glues empirically, this proposal directly challenges the field's foundational assumption by seeking to define intrinsic small-molecule binding principles for E3s, moving from serendipitous discovery to rational design. It applies Schreiber's expertise in chemical biology and small-molecule probes to a core, unexplored question in the TPD mechanism.

proposals.title: Structural Elucidation of RNF19A/B Stereospecificity for the PROTAC BRD1732 Informs the Design of Next-Generation Molecular Glues
proposals.motivation: Recent work from the Schreiber lab and others has demonstrated that small-molecule degraders, including PROTACs and molecular glues, can induce highly specific intracellular ubiquitination of target proteins, offering a powerful therapeutic strategy. The E3 ligases RNF19A and RNF19B have been identified as key mediators of this process for compounds like BRD1732, but the precise structural basis for their stereospecific recognition of the degrader and subsequent ubiquitin transfer remains unknown. Understanding this mechanism is critical for rationally designing degraders with improved selectivity and potency, particularly for challenging targets like mutant p53.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the stereospecificity of BRD1732 for RNF19A/B is governed by distinct, high-affinity binding pockets within the RING domains or associated substrate recognition modules, and that solving these ternary complex structures will reveal conserved molecular features essential for productive ubiquitination.
proposals.method: We will express and purify full-length human RNF19A and RNF19B, along with the E2 enzyme UbcH5c, using baculovirus and mammalian expression systems. Ternary complexes (E3-BRD1732-E2~Ub) will be assembled using the active, stereopure BRD1732 enantiomer and a non-hydrolyzable ubiquitin analog. High-resolution crystal structures will be determined via X-ray crystallography, employing molecular replacement with known RING domain structures as initial search models. Computational analysis, including molecular dynamics simulations (using AMBER) and binding free energy calculations (MM-GBSA), will be used to validate the structural models and quantify stereospecific interactions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first atomic-resolution view of how RNF19 family E3 ligases engage a small-molecule degrader, directly informing the structure-based design of novel molecular glues and PROTACs. It will establish a generalizable structural framework for understanding E3 ligase stereospecificity, a major bottleneck in degrader development. The findings will advance the field of targeted protein degradation by enabling the exploitation of underutilized E3 ligases for therapeutic intervention.
proposals.novelty: While structures of other E3 ligases (e.g., VHL, CRBN) with degraders exist, the structural biology of the RNF19 family in complex with a small-molecule recruiter is entirely unexplored. Determining the basis for its unique stereospecificity represents a significant and novel step beyond current empirical degrader discovery.

proposals.title: Fragment-Based Mapping of the Ubiquitin Transfer Active Site in E3 Ligases Using Chemoproteomic Detection of Intracellular Fragment Ubiquitination
proposals.motivation: E3 ubiquitin ligases are highly attractive but challenging therapeutic targets due to their role in directing protein degradation and cellular signaling. While Schreiber's lab and others have developed proximity-inducing 'molecular glues' and PROTACs that recruit E3s, a fundamental gap exists in understanding the minimal chemical features these enzymes recognize for direct, small-molecule ubiquitination. Mapping the E3 active site for small molecule substrates would provide a blueprint for rational design of next-generation degraders and E3-targeted therapeutics.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that high-throughput, fragment-based drug discovery (FBDD) screens, coupled with sensitive chemoproteomic detection of intracellular ubiquitination, can identify minimal chemical fragments that are direct substrates for specific E3 ligases, thereby revealing key pharmacophores and mapping the small-molecule-binding landscape of the E3 catalytic site.
proposals.method: We will employ a target-agnostic FBDD screen using a diverse, low-molecular-weight (<250 Da) fragment library. Intracellular ubiquitination of fragments will be detected using a tandem mass-tag (TMT) multiplexed proteomics workflow, building on Schreiber's prior method for detecting small-molecule ubiquitination. Hits will be validated using orthogonal assays, including ubiquitin-chain formation assays and competition with known E3 substrates. Co-crystallization or cryo-EM of fragment-bound E3 ligase complexes (e.g., CRBN, DCAF15) will be pursued to obtain atomic-resolution structural maps of the binding sites.
proposals.contribution: This work will generate the first 'ubiquitinome' map for chemical fragments, directly linking fragment structure to E3 catalytic activity. It will provide unprecedented structural insight into the E3 active site, moving beyond protein-protein interfaces to small-molecule recognition. The resulting fragment 'pharmacophore map' will serve as a foundational resource for designing more potent and selective molecular glues, PROTACs, and direct E3 inhibitors.
proposals.novelty: While FBDD is established for enzyme inhibitors, its application to systematically discover and map minimal *substrates* for ubiquitination by E3 ligases is novel. This approach inverts the typical paradigm, using the enzyme's catalytic function as the primary readout to deconstruct its recognition logic, rather than seeking inhibitory binding.

proposals.title: Activity-Based Profiling of Deubiquitinase Dysfunction Under Conditions of Ubiquitin Monomer Stress
proposals.motivation: Ubiquitin homeostasis is critical for proteostasis and cell signaling, yet the cellular consequences of free ubiquitin monomer accumulation are poorly understood. This state, potentially induced by proteasome inhibition, translation stress, or specific genetic lesions, could dysregulate the ubiquitin-proteasome system (UPS). Pre-2026 work from Schreiber's lab and others has developed small molecules that manipulate ubiquitination, but a proteome-wide view of the enzymatic vulnerabilities caused by ubiquitin monomer excess is lacking.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that accumulation of free ubiquitin monomers will selectively inhibit a subset of deubiquitinases (DUBs) via substrate competition and allosteric mechanisms, and that these inhibited DUBs can be identified using activity-based protein profiling (ABPP) with ubiquitin-vinyl sulfone (Ub-VS) probes.
proposals.method: We will treat diverse cell lines (cancer, primary) with established perturbations to elevate free ubiquitin monomers (e.g., proteasome inhibitors, translation puromycylation). Cell lysates will be profiled using pan-reactive HA-tagged Ub-VS and more selective Ub-based ABP probes to covalently label active-site cysteine DUBs. Probe-labeled enzymes will be enriched via streptavidin pulldown, identified by tandem mass spectrometry (LC-MS/MS), and quantified relative to controls. Hits will be validated using orthogonal enzymatic assays and cellular readouts of DUB substrate turnover.
proposals.contribution: This work will map the DUB activity landscape under ubiquitin monomer stress, identifying novel regulatory nodes in the UPS. It will provide a mechanistic link between ubiquitin pool imbalances and specific signaling outcomes, potentially revealing new therapeutic targets for diseases characterized by proteostatic dysfunction, such as neurodegeneration or cancer.
proposals.novelty: While ABPP with Ub-VS probes is established, its application to systematically profile DUB activity changes under defined states of ubiquitin monomer imbalance is novel. This approach moves beyond cataloging DUB expression to directly measuring functional activity in a disease-relevant proteostatic context.

proposals.title: High-Throughput Discovery of RNF19A/B Substrates via DNA-Encoded Library Screening to Enable Targeted Protein Ubiquitination
proposals.motivation: The ability to induce targeted protein degradation via proteolysis-targeting chimeras (PROTACs) has transformed chemical biology and therapeutics, but it relies on hijacking a limited set of E3 ligases. Directly recruiting the ubiquitination machinery to specific proteins via small molecules, as demonstrated for p53, remains a major challenge due to the scarcity of known E3-substrate pairs. RNF19A and RNF19B (Dorfin and RNF19B) are under-explored RBR-family E3 ligases implicated in Parkinson's disease and protein quality control, yet their native small-molecule substrates are unknown, limiting their utility as therapeutic handles.
proposals.hypothesis: DNA-encoded chemical libraries (DELs) can be screened against purified RNF19A/B E3 ligases to efficiently identify novel, ubiquitinatable small-molecule scaffolds that serve as specific chemical starting points for proximity-induced ubiquitination.
proposals.method: We will express and purify active, full-length human RNF19A and RNF19B. A large, diverse DEL (containing >1 billion compounds) will be incubated with the immobilized E3 ligase in the presence of E1, E2 (UbcH7), and biotinylated ubiquitin. Compounds that are ubiquitinated will be captured via streptavidin pulldown, and their DNA barcodes will be amplified and sequenced for identification. Hit compounds will be synthesized off-DNA and validated in biochemical ubiquitination assays and cellular models using ubiquitin-specific antibodies and mass spectrometry to confirm target engagement and ubiquitin transfer.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first high-throughput platform for directly discovering small-molecule substrates of specific E3 ligases. It will deliver novel, validated chemical scaffolds that are ubiquitinated by RNF19A/B, providing essential tool compounds to probe their biology and pioneering a generalizable DEL-based method to expand the E3 ligase toolbox for targeted protein modulation.
proposals.novelty: While DELs are used for target binding, applying them to directly screen for enzymatic *substrates* (ubiquitinatable compounds) of an E3 ligase, rather than inhibitors, is a novel reversal of the typical screening paradigm. This approach specifically targets the underutilized RNF19A/B ligases to uncover their latent small-molecule recognition capacity.

proposals.title: Minimal, Subcellularly-Targeted Ubiquitin-Sequestering Probes for Compartment-Specific Functional Interrogation
proposals.motivation: Ubiquitin (Ub) signaling is compartmentalized and essential for diverse cellular processes, but tools to manipulate Ub pools with spatial precision are lacking. Current methods, like proteasome inhibitors or E1 inhibitors, cause global disruption, obscuring compartment-specific Ub functions. A minimal, cell-permeable chemical probe that acts as a localized Ub 'sink' would enable unprecedented dissection of Ub-dependent pathways in organelles like the nucleus, mitochondria, or ER.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a minimal, non-proteinaceous chemical scaffold can be engineered to be efficiently and specifically ubiquitinated by endogenous E2/E3 machinery within cells, thereby creating a local depletion of free Ub in a targetable subcellular compartment without global proteotoxic stress.
proposals.method: We will design and synthesize a library of small (<500 Da), cell-permeable molecules featuring a core lysine-mimetic warhead optimized for E2/E3 recognition, fused to a heterobifunctional anchor for compartment-specific localization (e.g., using HaloTag or SNAP-tag technology, or mitochondrial/ER-targeting motifs). High-throughput screening in engineered cell lines with a FRET-based free Ub sensor will identify lead compounds that deplete free Ub. Specificity and mechanism will be validated using ubiquitination assays, mass spectrometry to map poly-Ub chain linkage, and CRISPR screens to identify required E3 ligases.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first class of chemical tools for spatially controlled manipulation of free Ub pools, enabling the study of compartment-specific ubiquitination in homeostasis and disease. It will establish a novel 'chemical glue' paradigm where the probe glues Ub to itself, rather than a target protein to an E3, opening a new avenue for probe and potential therapeutic development.
proposals.novelty: While Schreiber's lab has pioneered small molecules that induce intracellular ubiquitination of target proteins, the concept of a minimal, ubiquitinatable 'sponge' probe to sequester Ub locally is a distinct and unexplored strategy. It inverts the PROTAC paradigm by targeting the ubiquitin molecule itself for spatial control, rather than a specific protein for degradation.

proposals.title: Discovery of Small-Molecule Enhancers of Ubiquitin Chain Polymerization via a High-Throughput Biochemical Screen
proposals.motivation: The ubiquitin-proteasome system is a central regulator of protein homeostasis, yet most chemical probes and therapeutic strategies focus on inhibiting E3 ligases or the proteasome. This leaves a significant gap: the lack of tools to selectively *activate* ubiquitination pathways. Such molecules would be invaluable for chemical biology to study signal amplification and for targeted protein degradation (TPD) strategies where enhancing the ubiquitination of a neo-substrate could increase efficacy.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific small molecules can act as molecular glues or allosteric enhancers to stimulate the activity of E1, E2, or E3 enzymes or directly promote ubiquitin chain elongation, thereby increasing the rate and processivity of polyubiquitin chain formation.
proposals.method: We will develop a fluorescence polarization (FP) or time-resolved FRET (TR-FRET) high-throughput screen monitoring the elongation of a fluorescently labeled ubiquitin chain in a reconstituted biochemical system containing E1, a selected E2 (e.g., UbcH5c), and an E3 (e.g., a RING-type ligase). A diverse library of small molecules, including Schreiber's diversity-oriented synthesis (DOS) compounds, will be screened for compounds that increase the rate of signal change. Hits will be validated in orthogonal assays, including ubiquitin discharge assays and mass spectrometry to confirm enhanced chain formation. Structure-activity relationship (SAR) studies will be conducted to develop chemical probes.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first chemical probes that enhance ubiquitin polymerization, providing new tools to dissect the kinetics and regulation of the ubiquitination cascade. It will establish a novel screening paradigm for discovering enzymatic activators in ubiquitination and open a new frontier in TPD by enabling 'enhancer' molecules that could work synergistically with PROTACs or molecular glues.
proposals.novelty: While immense effort has been devoted to discovering ubiquitination inhibitors (e.g., MLN4924, proteasome inhibitors), a systematic search for small-molecule enhancers of the entire polymerization process is unprecedented. This directly inverts the prevailing chemical strategy in the field.

proposals.title: Discovery of Monovalent Small Molecules that Induce Target-Specific Intracellular Ubiquitination and Degradation
proposals.motivation: Targeted protein degradation via PROTACs and molecular glues has revolutionized chemical biology and therapeutics, but the field operates under a core assumption: degradation requires a heterobifunctional molecule or a glue to co-opt an E3 ligase. This paradigm limits chemical space and creates molecules with challenging pharmacokinetic properties. A systematic search for monovalent degraders could unlock new, simpler modalities for perturbing protein function.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that certain small molecules, binding a target protein with high specificity, can directly induce or stabilize a conformational state that is inherently recognizable by the cell's endogenous ubiquitin-proteasome system, leading to degradation without exogenous E3 ligase recruitment.
proposals.method: We will employ Schreiber's diversity-oriented synthesis (DOS) and phenotypic screening expertise. A DOS library will be screened in cells expressing a HaloTagged reporter of ubiquitination (inspired by pre-2026 'intracellular ubiquitination' work) coupled to a destabilized fluorescent protein. Hits inducing ubiquitination and loss of fluorescence will be counter-screened against E3 ligase knockout cell lines. Target identification will use affinity-based chemoproteomics and cellular thermal shift assays (CETSA). Structure-activity relationships will be developed to define the chemical features enabling monovalent degradation.
proposals.contribution: This work will challenge a foundational assumption in chemical biology by providing proof-of-concept for monovalent degraders. It will deliver novel chemical probes for historically 'undruggable' targets and establish a screening framework for discovering this new class of proximity-inducing molecules. The findings could simplify the design of future therapeutic degraders.
proposals.novelty: While PROTACs and glues are established, the deliberate search for monovalent degraders that act without designed E3 recruitment is non-obvious and underexplored. It merges the concepts of conformational control, intrinsic protein quality control, and phenotypic screening in a novel way.

proposals.title: Elucidating the Ubiquitin Ligase Recruitment and Proteasomal Degradation Pathway Triggered by the Cysteine-Targeting Molecular Glue ZL-12A
proposals.motivation: Targeted protein degradation via molecular glues offers a powerful therapeutic strategy for previously 'undruggable' proteins, but the mechanisms by which small molecules induce neo-protein-protein interactions remain poorly understood. ZL-12A, which binds C342 of ERCC3, represents a unique tool to dissect how a covalent, site-specific interaction can rewire the ubiquitin-proteasome system. Understanding this precise mechanism is critical for rationally designing next-generation glues and for predicting on-target and off-target effects in therapeutic contexts.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that ZL-12A binding to C342 of ERCC3 induces a conformational change or creates a novel molecular surface that is selectively recognized by a specific E3 ubiquitin ligase complex, leading to processive polyubiquitination and proteasomal degradation.
proposals.method: We will employ a multi-faceted approach combining chemoproteomics and functional genomics. First, we will use affinity purification of biotinylated ZL-12A probes coupled with mass spectrometry to identify proteins that co-precipitate with the drug-ERCC3 complex. Second, we will perform a genome-wide CRISPR-Cas9 knockout screen in cells treated with ZL-12A to identify genes essential for ERCC3 degradation, focusing on E3 ligases and proteasome components. Third, we will validate hits using co-immunoprecipitation and in vitro ubiquitination assays with recombinant proteins.
proposals.contribution: This work will define the complete cellular degradation pathway for a covalent molecular glue, identifying the key E3 ligase and adaptor proteins involved. It will establish a generalizable framework for mechanistically studying other targeted degraders and provide critical insights into the structural features required for induced proximity. The findings will directly inform the design of more selective and potent degradation-based chemical probes and therapeutics.
proposals.novelty: While PROTACs are well-studied, the mechanism of action for monovalent, covalent 'molecular glues' like ZL-12A that degrade without an obvious bifunctional design is largely unexplored. This study moves beyond phenotypic observation to a definitive, mechanistic dissection of the entire degradation cascade initiated by a single-site covalent modification.

proposals.title: Structural and Mechanistic Basis for the Mutual Antagonism of ZL-12A and Triptolide in Targeted Protein Degradation
proposals.motivation: Targeted protein degradation via molecular glues and PROTACs is a transformative therapeutic modality, but its efficacy can be compromised by unanticipated drug-drug interactions. The observed cross-antagonism between the cereblon-based molecular glue ZL-12A and the natural product triptolide, which modulates the CRL4CRBN E3 ligase complex, presents a critical challenge. Understanding this interference is essential for rationally designing combination therapies and predicting clinical outcomes in oncology and beyond.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that ZL-12A and triptolide engage in a mutually exclusive, allosteric competition for binding to the cereblon-DDB1 complex, leading to distinct, antagonistic conformational states that either promote or inhibit the recruitment and ubiquitination of neo-substrates.
proposals.method: We will employ a multi-pronged approach: 1) Structural biology using cryo-EM to solve the ternary complexes of CRL4CRBN bound to ZL-12A or triptolide, identifying key binding interfaces and conformational changes. 2) Cellular profiling via multiplexed quantitative proteomics (TMT-MS) to map the global protein degradation signatures of each compound alone and in combination, quantifying antagonism. 3) Biochemical assays including surface plasmon resonance (SPR) and in vitro ubiquitination to measure binding kinetics and enzymatic activity of the reconstituted complexes under competitive conditions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first atomic-resolution model of a small-molecule antagonist of a molecular glue E3 ligase complex, defining a new class of pharmacological interference. It will establish a mechanistic framework for predicting off-target interactions in degradation-based therapies and inform the design of next-generation glues with reduced susceptibility to antagonism.
proposals.novelty: While molecular glue mechanisms are being elucidated, the specific structural basis for direct antagonism between two small molecules targeting the same E3 ligase complex remains entirely unexplored. This moves beyond characterizing single agents to defining the rules of engagement within a shared macromolecular machine.

proposals.title: Cellular Kinetic Profiling of Stereoprobes Reveals Rate-Limiting Steps in Covalent Target Engagement at Cysteines
proposals.motivation: Covalent small molecules, including Schreiber's stereoprobes, offer high selectivity but their therapeutic potential depends on the kinetics of target engagement within the complex cellular milieu. Pre-2026, profiling has focused on equilibrium binding or endpoint measurements, missing critical kinetic parameters like on-rates (k_on) and residence times that govern efficacy, selectivity, and off-target effects in living cells. Understanding these dynamics is essential for rationally designing next-generation covalent molecular glues and targeted degraders.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the cellular kinetic profiles of stereoprobes—specifically their association rates and residence times at target cysteines—are major determinants of their functional selectivity and antiproliferative activity, and that these parameters can be quantitatively measured and engineered.
proposals.method: We will employ cellular kinetic target engagement assays using pulse-chase competitive activity-based protein profiling (ABPP) with cysteine-reactive probes like iodoacetamide-alkyne. Cells treated with stereoprobes for varying times will be lysed, and remaining free target cysteines will be labeled with the ABPP probe for click-chemistry-based quantification via mass spectrometry. Kinetic parameters (k_obs, k_on, k_off) will be derived by globally fitting time- and concentration-dependent labeling data to a kinetic model of covalent inhibition using established algorithms (e.g., in GraphPad Prism or custom Python scripts using SciPy for nonlinear regression). Residence times will be calculated as 1/k_off.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a generalizable, cell-based framework for measuring the kinetic signatures of covalent small molecules, moving beyond static affinity measurements. It will provide unprecedented insight into how binding kinetics translate to functional outcomes in Schreiber's stereoprobe platform, directly informing the design of molecular glues with optimized temporal control over target modulation.
proposals.novelty: While ABPP is established for target identification, its application for deriving full kinetic parameters (k_on, k_off) of small molecules in intact cells is novel. This approach integrates the principles of covalent drug kinetics with the complexity of the cellular proteome, a significant advance over purified enzyme assays.

proposals.title: Development of Molecular Glues to Specifically Inhibit Oncogenic Protein Reinitiation Pathways
proposals.motivation: Targeting de novo protein synthesis for cancer therapy is challenging due to essential cellular functions and toxicity. However, many oncoproteins, like mutant p53, are not only synthesized de novo but are also actively re-synthesized or 'reinitiated' following stress or degradation, a process critical for tumor survival. Selective inhibition of this reinitiation pathway could offer a therapeutic window not achievable by targeting fundamental biosynthesis.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the molecular machinery governing the reinitiation of specific oncogenic proteins is distinct from de novo synthesis and can be selectively targeted using proximity-inducing small molecules (molecular glues) to induce degradation or inhibit translation of the reinitiating protein complex.
proposals.method: We will employ a chemical biology approach, screening Schreiber's diversity-oriented synthesis libraries for compounds that selectively reduce levels of a reinitiation reporter (e.g., a destabilized mutant p53-GFP fusion) but not a constitutive control, in isogenic cell lines. Hits will be optimized and their mechanism elucidated using Chemoproteomics (e.g., isotopic tandem orthogonal proteolysis-activity-based protein profiling, isoTOP-ABPP) to identify the bound protein target. Ternary complex formation and inhibition of reinitiation will be validated using cellular thermal shift assays (CETSA) and ribosome profiling.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver first-in-class chemical probes that selectively disrupt oncoprotein reinitiation, validating it as a druggable pathway. It will expand the toolkit of molecular glues beyond traditional degradation to translational control, providing a novel strategy for targeting 'undruggable' oncoproteins with mutant-specific dependencies.
proposals.novelty: While molecular glues have been used to degrade proteins, their application to specifically inhibit the reinitiation phase of protein synthesis, a process distinct from steady-state abundance, is unexplored. This moves beyond targeting protein stability to targeting the context-specific resynthesis that fuels cancer adaptation.

proposals.title: Bifunctional Molecular Glues for Targeted Nuclear Export of Pathogenic Proteins
proposals.motivation: Many diseases, including cancer and neurodegeneration, are driven by the aberrant nuclear localization of pathogenic proteins (e.g., mutant p53, tau, or transcription factors). Current genetic or RNA-based methods to deplete these proteins lack temporal control and tissue specificity. A chemical strategy to force their export and subsequent cytosolic degradation would offer a powerful, tunable therapeutic modality, complementing recent advances in targeted protein degradation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that bifunctional small molecules, designed to simultaneously bind a target nuclear protein and a cytosolic export carrier (e.g., CRM1/XPO1 or a non-canonical transporter), can be engineered to induce selective nuclear export, rendering the protein accessible to cytosolic quality control or degradation machinery.
proposals.method: We will employ a structure-informed design of heterobifunctional compounds linking a target-binding warhead (e.g., for mutant p53 or a disease-relevant transcription factor) to a high-affinity ligand for a cytosolic export factor. Candidate molecules will be screened in cell-based assays monitoring nuclear-to-cytosolic protein translocation via immunofluorescence and fractionation. Hits will be optimized using Schreiber's diversity-oriented synthesis (DOS) principles to improve permeability, selectivity, and export efficiency. A proteomics-based strategy (e.g., APEX tagging) will validate on-target engagement and identify any co-exported complexes.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new chemical platform for controlling protein localization, termed 'export-targeting chimeras' (ExTACs). It will provide proof-of-concept for a novel therapeutic strategy distinct from degradation, potentially applicable to 'undruggable' nuclear proteins. The project will also deliver novel chemical probes to dissect nuclear export pathways.
proposals.novelty: While PROTACs and molecular glues induce degradation, the direct hijacking of export machinery to forcibly relocate proteins from the nucleus is an unexplored application of bifunctional chemistry. This approach targets a protein's compartmentalization, not its stability, offering a unique mechanistic angle.

proposals.title: Inducing Target Proximity with Monovalent Small Molecules and Engineered Dimerizing Peptides
proposals.motivation: Bifunctional PROTACs and molecular glues have revolutionized targeted protein degradation and modulation, but their reliance on a single small molecule to bind both target and effector protein imposes significant design constraints. This necessitates extensive linker optimization and can limit the scope of 'undruggable' targets that can be engaged. Exploring alternative, modular strategies for induced co-localization could expand the chemical and target space for proximity-based therapeutics.
proposals.hypothesis: A monovalent, target-binding small molecule can be functionally converted into a proximity-inducing agent by genetically fusing its cognate protein target to a chemically induced dimerization (CID) domain, which is then engaged by a separate, orthogonal dimerizing peptide or small molecule.
proposals.method: We will engineer a fusion protein comprising a protein of interest (POI) and a FKBP12F36V domain. A cell-permeable, monovalent small molecule binder of the POI will be synthesized or repurposed. Separately, we will employ the dTAG system's bifunctional molecule, which binds FKBP12F36V and recruits an E3 ligase, but here it will act on the engineered fusion, not the native POI. This decouples target engagement from effector recruitment. As a parallel, light-controlled modality, we will fuse the POI to Cry2 and express CIBN-tagged effectors, using blue light to trigger reversible co-localization, benchmarking against the chemical system.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a generalizable 'tag-and-dimerize' platform that separates the challenges of finding a high-affinity target binder from optimizing proximity induction. It will provide a comparative analysis of chemical versus optical dimerization for controlling intracellular protein interactions, offering new toolkits for chemical biology and potentially new therapeutic modalities with improved selectivity profiles.
proposals.novelty: While CID systems exist, their application to redirect a monovalent, drug-like small molecule's function from inhibition to proximity-based regulation by genetically 'hijacking' its target is unexplored. This challenges the necessity of a bifunctional small molecule architecture by distributing its functions across two separate, modular components.

proposals.title: Cytosolic Hijacking of Nuclear Transcription Factors via Bifunctional Molecular Glues for Cancer Therapy
proposals.motivation: The mislocalization of nuclear proteins is a powerful but underexplored therapeutic strategy. Current proximity-inducing platforms, like PROTACs, primarily target proteins for degradation, but forced relocalization offers a distinct, potentially reversible mechanism to disrupt oncogenic transcription factor networks. This approach could provide a new therapeutic window for cancers driven by transcription factors historically considered 'undruggable'.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a bifunctional small molecule, designed as a molecular glue, can co-opt an abundant, stable cytosolic 'carrier' protein to sequester and mislocalize a specific nuclear transcription factor to the cytoplasm, thereby inhibiting its oncogenic transcriptional program.
proposals.method: We will employ a chemical proteomics strategy to identify small molecules that bind a chosen cytosolic anchor protein (e.g., FKBP12). Using structure-based design, we will synthesize bifunctional derivatives linking this anchor-binding pharmacophore to moieties known to engage the target nuclear protein (e.g., a mutant p53 stabilizer). High-content imaging with subcellular fractionation and NanoBRET assays will quantify mislocalization efficiency. Functional impact will be assessed via RNA-seq and viability assays in relevant cancer cell lines.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a generalizable 'inverted carrier' platform for the spatial control of protein function, distinct from degradation. It will deliver a first-in-class chemical probe to validate cytoplasmic sequestration of a nuclear oncoprotein as a viable cancer therapeutic strategy, expanding the toolbox of proximity-inducing medicines.
proposals.novelty: This inverts the classic cargo-carrier paradigm by using a cytosolic protein as the dominant localization driver, a concept distinct from appending nuclear export signals. It applies the molecular glue principle not for degradation, but for engineered subcellular sequestration, a novel application of bifunctional chemistry.

proposals.title: In Vivo Validation of Bifunctional p53-Stabilizing Molecular Glues in TP53 Mutant Cancer Models
proposals.motivation: TP53 mutations are among the most common genetic alterations in human cancers, often leading to loss of tumor suppressor function and poor prognosis. Pre-2026 work from Schreiber's lab and others has demonstrated that bifunctional 'molecular glue' compounds can rescue p53 function by inducing proximity between mutant p53 and ubiquitin ligase machinery, leading to nuclear import and stabilization. However, the therapeutic efficacy and pharmacokinetic feasibility of this protein-targeting chimera (PROTAC)-like strategy remain unproven in physiologically relevant animal models.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that optimized bifunctional compounds inducing nuclear import and stabilization of mutant p53 will demonstrate significant anti-tumor activity and acceptable tolerability in vivo, validating the p53 protein abundance window as a druggable target.
proposals.method: We will employ a panel of well-characterized, immunocompromised mouse xenograft models derived from human cancer cell lines harboring diverse TP53 hotspot mutations (e.g., R175H, R273H). Lead bifunctional compounds, previously validated for intracellular ubiquitination and p53 stabilization in vitro, will be administered via systemic routes. Tumor growth will be monitored longitudinally, and pharmacodynamic endpoints will be assessed via immunohistochemistry and Western blot of excised tumors to quantify nuclear p53 accumulation, downstream target activation (e.g., p21), and apoptosis markers. Parallel studies will evaluate compound pharmacokinetics and preliminary toxicity.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first in vivo proof-of-concept for a proximity-inducing small molecule strategy targeting mutant p53, a major unmet need in oncology. It will establish critical parameters for translating intracellular protein stabilization into a viable therapeutic modality and identify lead candidates for further development.
proposals.novelty: While PROTACs for degrading oncoproteins are established, using bifunctional compounds to *stabilize and reactivate* a specific mutant tumor suppressor protein in vivo is a novel therapeutic paradigm. This moves beyond cell-based studies to test a fundamental premise of the 'molecular glue' approach for gain-of-function rescue.

proposals.title: Optogenetic Control of Cytosolic Protein Abundance Using a Re-engineered LOVTRAP System for Dynamic Functional Profiling
proposals.motivation: The ability to rapidly and reversibly control the localization and abundance of specific proteins in living cells is a powerful tool for probing protein function and validating therapeutic targets. Pre-2026 optogenetic tools like LOVTRAP, which uses the LOV2 domain of phototropin 1 and its binding partner Zdark, enable light-dependent sequestration of proteins to the plasma membrane, but this membrane-tethered approach is suboptimal for studying cytosolic and nuclear proteins. A method for reversible, light-controlled sequestration within the cytosol itself would provide superior spatiotemporal precision for interrogating signaling pathways and protein-protein interactions without disrupting membrane-associated processes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that engineering a cytosolic anchor scaffold fused to the Zdark peptide will enable the rapid, reversible, and light-dependent sequestration of LOV2-tagged cargo proteins within the cytosol, creating a tunable 'sink' to modulate functional protein concentration with high temporal resolution.
proposals.method: We will engineer a cytosolic LOVTRAP system by fusing the Zdark peptide to a self-assembling, inert protein scaffold (e.g., a modified ferritin cage or a repeat-protein oligomer) to create a multivalent cytosolic anchor (CytoZ). The cargo protein will be fused to the LOV2 domain. Under blue light, the LOV2 domain will undergo a conformational change, exposing its Jα-helix to bind CytoZ, sequestering the cargo. Removal of light will reverse the binding. We will validate the system using model cargoes (e.g., GFP, p53) in HEK293T cells, quantifying sequestration kinetics via fluorescence recovery after photobleaching (FRAP) and functional consequences via downstream reporter assays.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a new optogenetic tool for the precise, reversible control of cytosolic and nuclear protein availability, directly complementing Schreiber's work on proximity-inducing molecules and targeted protein degradation. It will enable acute, temporal studies of protein function, mimicking the acute pharmacological effects of molecular glues and PROTACs but with light-switchable precision, providing a critical orthogonal method for target validation and pathway dissection.
proposals.novelty: While LOVTRAP is established for membrane sequestration, re-engineering it for high-capacity, reversible capture within the cytosol is a novel application. This addresses a key limitation by avoiding membrane tethering, thereby enabling the study of a broader class of proteins involved in cytosolic signaling and complex formation without perturbing their native membrane interactions.

proposals.title: Systematic Profiling of FKBP12-Directed Molecular Glues Reveals Unexpected Protein Degradation Landscapes
proposals.motivation: Targeted protein degradation via molecular glues offers a transformative therapeutic strategy for previously 'undruggable' proteins. However, the discovery of new glues remains largely serendipitous, and their selectivity profiles are poorly understood. A systematic, unbiased assessment of the degradation potential of a well-characterized glue recruiter like FKBP12 against a broad proteome is critical to map the true scope and specificity of this modality, informing both therapeutic design and fundamental biology.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that screening FKBP12-directed molecular glues against a diverse, large-scale protein panel will reveal novel, high-specificity degradation events beyond known neo-substrates, uncovering new biological nodes susceptible to proximity-induced pharmacology.
proposals.method: We will employ DNA-encoded library (DEL) technology, a core Schreiber lab strength, to screen FKBP12-binding molecular glues against a panel of 500+ E3 ligase-substrate fusion proteins, representing diverse structural families and disease-relevant targets. Hits will be validated in cells using quantitative proteomics (e.g., TMT-MS) and orthogonal assays (e.g., NanoBRET, immunoblotting) to confirm degradation kinetics and specificity. Structure-activity relationship (SAR) studies will be conducted on validated hits to optimize glue potency and selectivity.
proposals.contribution: This work will generate the first large-scale map of degradation events initiated from a single recruiter protein, establishing a foundational resource for the field. It will identify new therapeutic targets amenable to glue-based degradation and provide critical empirical data on the 'rules' governing molecular glue specificity, moving the field beyond anecdotal discovery.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has identified individual FKBP12-mediated degraders, a systematic, proteome-wide screen for degradation events from this node has not been conducted. This approach moves from targeted validation to unbiased discovery, directly testing the latent promiscuity or specificity of a foundational glue system.

proposals.title: Systematic Discovery of Intracellular Presenters for a Fixed Target Protein via Bifunctional Small Molecule Screening
proposals.motivation: Targeted protein degradation via PROTACs and molecular glues has revolutionized chemical biology and therapeutics, but these approaches typically start with a known E3 ligase presenter. The inverse problem—identifying which of the hundreds of intracellular proteins can be recruited as presenters for a given disease-relevant target—remains largely unexplored. Systematically mapping these interactions would unlock new therapeutic modalities and expand the druggable proteome beyond traditional enzyme inhibition.
proposals.hypothesis: For a specific, therapeutically relevant target protein, a diverse library of heterobifunctional small molecules, each bearing a target-binding warhead linked to variable, low-affinity 'presenter-recruiting' elements, will reveal a broad set of endogenous intracellular proteins capable of functioning as proximity-induced presenters, leading to novel pharmacological outcomes.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a focused library of ~500 bifunctional compounds, each featuring a validated, high-affinity ligand for a selected target protein (e.g., mutant p53) conjugated via a flexible linker to a diverse array of low-molecular-weight 'presenter-recruiting' fragments. This library will be screened in live cells expressing the target protein using a combination of affinity-based proteomics (e.g., Chemoproteomics with TMT labeling) and functional readouts (e.g., NanoBRET for proximity or ubiquitination). Hits will be validated by CRISPRi knockdown of candidate presenter proteins and mechanistic follow-up via immunoprecipitation and mass spectrometry.
proposals.contribution: This work will generate the first systematic map of potential presenter proteins for a high-value target, moving beyond the limited set of known E3 ligases. It will establish a generalizable screening platform for the 'inverse problem' in proximity-inducing pharmacology and likely identify novel, tissue-specific presenters that could enable new therapeutic strategies with improved selectivity.
proposals.novelty: While previous work has focused on optimizing glues for known presenter-target pairs, this proposal inverts the paradigm by fixing the target and screening for novel presenters, a fundamentally unexplored strategy in chemical biology. It leverages Schreiber's expertise in diversity-oriented synthesis and phenotypic screening to probe cellular function in an unbiased manner.

proposals.title: Bifunctional Molecular Glues for Targeted Protein Activation and Complex Assembly
proposals.motivation: The field of targeted protein degradation, exemplified by PROTACs and molecular glues, has demonstrated the power of proximity-inducing molecules to eliminate disease-causing proteins. However, this represents only one functional outcome (degradation) of induced proximity. Pre-2026, there is a significant unmet opportunity to design bifunctional compounds that trigger novel, therapeutically relevant functions—such as targeted activation, allosteric modulation, or the *de novo* assembly of multi-protein complexes—to address undruggable targets and create new biological tools.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by rationally linking a target-binding ligand to a recruiter ligand for a specific effector protein or complex, we can create bifunctional molecules that induce proximity to drive novel, predictable functional outcomes beyond degradation, such as targeted post-translational modification or complex stabilization.
proposals.method: We will employ a structure-informed design strategy, leveraging pre-2026 structural databases (PDB) and computational docking (using Rosetta or Schrödinger Suite) to model ternary complex formation. A modular synthetic chemistry platform will be used to conjugate target-binding warheads (e.g., from fragment screens or known inhibitors) to recruiter ligands for specific E3 ligase catalytic domains (for ubiquitination), acetyltransferases (for acetylation), or other enzymatic "effectors." High-content cellular screening with fluorescent protein reporters and automated microscopy will be used to identify functional outcomes (e.g., altered localization, modified activity) rather than mere degradation.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a generalizable design framework for bifunctional molecules with programmable functions, moving the field beyond degradation. It will deliver first-in-class chemical tools for targeted protein activation and complex assembly, providing novel probes for biological discovery and new therapeutic modalities for targets where loss-of-function is not desirable.
proposals.novelty: While existing bifunctionals (PROTACs) are designed for target elimination, this proposal focuses on inducing gain-of-function or novel regulatory events through proximity, a conceptually distinct and largely unexplored application of the molecular glue paradigm.

proposals.title: Bifunctional Molecular Glues for the De Novo Assembly and Stabilization of Endogenous Multi-Protein Complexes
proposals.motivation: Molecular glues have revolutionized targeted protein degradation by inducing proximity between an E3 ligase and a target protein. However, their application has been largely limited to binary interactions. Many essential cellular functions, from transcription to signal transduction, are governed by large, multi-subunit complexes whose dysregulation is central to disease. The ability to rationally nucleate or stabilize such endogenous complexes with small molecules would open a new frontier in chemical biology and therapeutics, moving beyond degradation to controlled assembly.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that bifunctional small molecules, designed with dual, weak-affinity binding surfaces, can selectively co-recruit specific endogenous protein components to nucleate the formation of higher-order complexes, thereby modulating their function in cells.
proposals.method: We will leverage Schreiber's expertise in diversity-oriented synthesis and phenotypic screening to create libraries of conformationally constrained, heterobifunctional compounds. Target complexes will be selected based on known but weak endogenous interactions (e.g., components of the SWI/SNF chromatin remodeling complex). High-throughput FRET and AlphaScreen assays will be developed to detect compound-induced complex formation in vitro. Hits will be validated using cellular thermal shift assays (CETSA) for target engagement, co-immunoprecipitation followed by mass spectrometry (CoIP-MS) to confirm complex assembly, and functional genomic readouts (e.g., RNA-seq) to assess biological consequence.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a first-in-class chemical toolkit for the direct assembly of multi-protein complexes, providing novel probes to dissect complex biology. It will expand the therapeutic modality of molecular glues from degradation to stabilization and creation of functional macromolecular machines, potentially targeting diseases of haploinsufficiency or complex instability.
proposals.novelty: While existing PROTACs and molecular glues induce degradation via binary proximity, this proposal aims to use small molecules as scaffolds for the de novo assembly of endogenous multi-protein structures, a significant conceptual and technical leap from current proximity-inducing technologies.

proposals.title: Design of Intramolecular Glues: Small-Molecule Inducers of Compaction in Intrinsically Disordered Proteins
proposals.motivation: Intrinsically disordered proteins (IDPs) and regions (IDRs) are prevalent in eukaryotic proteomes and central to signaling, regulation, and disease, yet they are notoriously difficult to target with conventional small molecules that require structured binding pockets. The Schreiber lab's pioneering work on molecular glues that induce proximity between two proteins suggests a novel, underexplored strategy: designing 'intramolecular glues' that induce proximity within a single protein chain, forcing a disordered region into a specific, stabilized conformation. This could create a new therapeutic modality for diseases driven by dysregulated IDPs, such as transcription factors in cancer or tau in neurodegeneration.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that small, bifunctional molecules can be designed to bind two distinct, low-affinity sites within an intrinsically disordered protein region, thereby inducing its compaction into a defined, functional, or degradation-prone state.
proposals.method: We will employ a target-centric, chemistry-first approach. First, we will use NMR and hydrogen-deuterium exchange mass spectrometry (HDX-MS) to map transient structural elements and potential cryptic pockets in model IDRs (e.g., from p53, c-Myc). Second, we will design and synthesize a library of conformationally constrained, bicyclic small molecules (inspired by the lab's work on pyrrolidine inhibitors) featuring variable linkers, aiming to mimic the distance and geometry between two mapped sites. Third, we will screen for compaction using a Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET)-based assay with fluorescent tags flanking the IDR. Hits will be validated by NMR for induced structural changes and assessed for functional consequences in cellular models.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a proof-of-concept for 'intramolecular glue' chemistry, providing a novel design principle for targeting the untapped proteome of disordered proteins. It will deliver first-in-class chemical probes that modulate IDP function via induced folding, offering new tools for chemical biology and a potential roadmap for therapeutic development against 'undruggable' targets.
proposals.novelty: While molecular glues for protein-protein dimerization are established, the concept of applying this paradigm intramolecularly to force a single protein into a specific fold is novel. This moves beyond stabilizing pre-existing interactions to creating entirely new, drug-induced conformational states within disordered regions.

proposals.title: A Structure-Guided, Protein-First Platform for the De Novo Design of Bifunctional Molecular Glues
proposals.motivation: Traditional phenotypic or target-based screening often yields poorly characterized hits or fails for 'undruggable' proteins. The emerging PROTAC and molecular glue paradigm demonstrates that inducing proximity is a powerful therapeutic strategy, but discovering such compounds remains largely serendipitous. A systematic, protein-first method to design proximity-inducing molecules for a chosen protein target would unlock a vast array of new therapeutic opportunities, particularly for disease-relevant proteins lacking conventional small-molecule binders.
proposals.hypothesis: By computationally prioritizing protein surface features amenable to neo-protein-protein interactions, we can rationally design or select small molecules that specifically bind a target protein and recruit a desired E3 ubiquitin ligase, thereby creating potent and selective degraders from first principles.
proposals.method: We will develop a computational pipeline integrating AlphaFold2 (pre-2026 state) for predicting target protein structures and potential ligase interfaces with molecular docking and fragment-based design. Starting with a protein of interest (e.g., a mutant p53 or a parasitic enzyme), the algorithm will identify 'druggable' surface pockets and propose linker geometries to connect a pocket-binding fragment to a known ligase-recruiting warhead (e.g., for CRBN or VHL). Synthesized candidates will be tested in cellular degradation assays, with iterative cycles of structural biology (cryo-EM/crystallography) and medicinal chemistry optimizing glue efficacy and specificity.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a generalizable, rational design platform for molecular glues, moving the field beyond serendipitous discovery. It will deliver novel, mechanistically validated chemical probes for specific proteins, demonstrating that virtually any protein can be targeted for degradation via a designed interface. The platform will be a transformative tool for chemical biology and therapeutic discovery.
proposals.novelty: This inverts the standard screening paradigm by beginning with a protein structure to computationally design a glue de novo, rather than screening compound libraries for degradation phenotypes. It combines pre-2026 deep learning-based structure prediction with the nascent concept of rational glue design, a largely unexplored frontier.

proposals.title: Structural and Energetic Determinants of Stereoselective Protein-Ligand Recognition for Small-Molecule Chemical Probes
proposals.motivation: The Schreiber lab and others have developed numerous stereoisomeric small molecules that act as chemical probes or molecular glues, exhibiting profound biological selectivity for one enantiomer over the other. This stereoselectivity is a powerful, yet poorly understood, design principle for achieving specificity in chemical biology and therapeutic discovery. A systematic structural understanding of the protein-ligand interactions underlying this phenomenon is lacking, limiting our ability to rationally design stereochemically defined probes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that stereoselective protein binding is governed by specific, high-resolution structural features and defined energetic contributions (e.g., hydrophobic packing, hydrogen bonding networks, and conformational water displacement) that can be systematically characterized and generalized across protein families.
proposals.method: We will select a curated panel of 8-10 well-characterized, stereoisomeric probe-target pairs from the lab's portfolio (e.g., BRD, kinase, or E3 ligase binders). For each pair, we will determine high-resolution co-crystal structures of the target protein bound to both the active and inactive enantiomer using X-ray crystallography. We will complement this with isothermal titration calorimetry (ITC) to quantify binding thermodynamics. Computational analysis will employ molecular dynamics simulations (using AMBER or GROMACS) and free energy perturbation (FEP) calculations to decompose the energetic contributions of individual interactions and solvation effects responsible for the observed selectivity.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic, structural, and thermodynamic framework for understanding stereoselective protein recognition in chemical biology. It will generate a set of design principles for exploiting chirality to enhance the specificity of future chemical probes and proximity-inducing molecules. The resulting high-resolution structural library will serve as a critical community resource for structure-based drug design.
proposals.novelty: While individual stereoselective interactions are known, a comparative structural-energetic analysis across diverse protein targets and probe chemotypes is unprecedented. This approach directly integrates Schreiber's expertise in probe discovery with structural biology and computational analysis to move from observation to predictive principle.

proposals.title: Proteome-Wide Profiling of Small Molecule Stereoselectivity Using Chemoproteomics and Bifunctional Degraders
proposals.motivation: Chirality is a fundamental property of bioactive small molecules, yet its impact on proteome-wide target engagement remains largely unmapped. Schreiber's work on molecular glues and proximity-inducing molecules has highlighted how subtle structural changes can dictate specificity. A systematic, quantitative map of stereoselectivity across the human proteome would transform our understanding of off-target effects, drug specificity, and the design of safer, more effective therapeutics.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that stereochemistry is a major, yet under-explored, determinant of proteome-wide small molecule interactions, and that systematic profiling will reveal novel, stereoselective protein targets and binding interfaces inaccessible to traditional screening.
