proposals.method: We will employ a chemoproteomic platform using stereoisomer pairs of cysteine-reactive, alkyne-functionalized small molecules as chemical probes. Following cell lysate treatment, probes will be conjugated to biotin via click chemistry, enriched on streptavidin beads, and identified by quantitative mass spectrometry (TMT or SILAC). This will be complemented by a functional screen using stereoisomer pairs of 'bifunctional degraders' (PROTACs) featuring different E3 ligase recruiters, monitoring protein degradation via a multiplexed, CRISPR-based transcriptional reporter system for protein stability (e.g., using PRISM or a similar cell array).
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first comprehensive, quantitative atlas of stereoselective protein-small molecule interactions, directly informing rational drug design. It will establish a generalizable platform for evaluating chirality in chemical biology probes and therapeutic candidates, and likely uncover new, stereochemically defined vulnerabilities for therapeutic intervention.
proposals.novelty: While chemoproteomics is established, its application to systematically compare enantiomer and diastereomer pairs across the entire proteome is novel. Combining this with a functional degradation readout for stereoisomeric PROTACs moves beyond mere binding to reveal stereoselectivity in a therapeutically relevant, phenotypic context.

proposals.title: Development of Covalent Antibody-Based Profilers (CAPs) for Chemoproteomic Mapping of Small Molecule Metabolites and Drugs
proposals.motivation: Current chemoproteomic methods for mapping small molecule-protein interactions, such as affinity-based protein profiling (ABPP), rely on modifying the small molecule with a chemical handle, which can alter its properties and limit in vivo applicability. A general, unbiased method to capture and identify diverse, unmodified small molecules directly from complex biological systems would revolutionize our understanding of drug metabolism, off-target effects, and the chemical space of the metabolome. This is a critical unmet need in chemical biology and drug discovery.
proposals.hypothesis: That engineered antibodies or protein scaffolds, equipped with catalytic nucleophiles and tailored for broad reactivity, can be designed to covalently capture a wide array of small molecule chemotypes, enabling their isolation, identification, and spatial mapping in cells.
proposals.method: We will employ yeast surface display libraries of single-chain variable fragments (scFvs) and designed ankyrin repeat proteins (DARPins) to select binders against diverse small molecule haptens. Selected binders will be engineered by site-directed mutagenesis to install reactive nucleophiles (e.g., cysteine, lysine, or non-canonical amino acids like 2,3-diaminopropionic acid) within the binding pocket to facilitate proximity-driven covalent capture. A parallel, structure-informed approach will use Rosetta-based computational design to graft reactive sites into existing protein scaffolds with pre-organized binding clefts. The most promising covalent profilers will be validated using mass spectrometry to identify captured ligands from spiked cell lysates and live cells.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel, protein-first platform for the unbiased discovery of small molecule interactions in native biological environments. It will provide a complementary tool to small molecule-centric chemoproteomics, enabling the profiling of metabolites and drugs without chemical modification. The resulting covalent antibody-based profilers (CAPs) could serve as foundational reagents for diagnostic and therapeutic applications.
proposals.novelty: While Schreiber's and others' work has focused on small molecules that recruit proteins (e.g., PROTACs, molecular glues), this proposal inverts the paradigm by designing proteins that actively capture small molecules. The systematic engineering of proteins for broad-spectrum, covalent small molecule capture, rather than high-affinity binding to a single target, is a distinct and unexplored strategy.

proposals.title: Systematic Mapping of Ligand-Repellent Protein Surfaces Using Chemoproteomic and Computational Analysis
proposals.motivation: The Schreiber lab's work on molecular glues and proximity-inducing molecules demonstrates that small molecules can engage challenging protein targets. However, a fundamental limitation in chemical biology and drug discovery is the 'undruggable' proteome, often attributed to a lack of suitable binding pockets. A systematic, empirical understanding of which protein surfaces actively repel small molecule binding, rather than merely lacking affinity, is missing. Mapping these 'ligand-repellent' regions would provide a critical negative design principle for developing new chemical probes and therapeutics.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific physicochemical and topological features of protein surfaces—beyond the absence of deep pockets—confer a generalized resistance to small molecule engagement, and that these features can be systematically identified through a combination of experimental saturation and computational analysis.
proposals.method: We will employ a two-pronged approach. First, we will use a chemoproteomic saturation screen with a diverse library of cysteine-reactive fragments, building on methods like isoTOP-ABPP, to empirically identify surface-exposed cysteines that remain unmodified despite high reagent concentration and accessibility. Second, we will integrate this experimental data with computational feature analysis using AlphaFold2 (pre-2026 version) to generate high-confidence structural models and Rosetta-based surface mapping. A machine learning model (e.g., a graph neural network) will be trained on the combined dataset to predict repellent features, such as specific charge distributions, hydration patterns, and shallow convex topology.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first experimentally grounded map of 'ligand-repellent' protein epitopes, moving beyond descriptive 'undruggability.' It will deliver a predictive computational model to flag resistant surfaces in proteins of interest. This will directly inform the design of new molecular glues and PROTACs by identifying avoidance zones and suggesting alternative recruitment strategies, thereby expanding the scope of addressable therapeutic targets.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies focus on finding bindable sites, this proposal inverts the problem to systematically define non-bindable regions using direct experimental observation. The integration of covalent fragment saturation screening with state-of-the-art structural prediction and machine learning to learn the rules of repellence is a novel and orthogonal strategy to conventional druggability assessments.

proposals.title: A Single-Well, Bimolecular Fluorescence Complementation (BiFC) Platform for the Discovery of Molecular Glues and Ternary Complex Inducers
proposals.motivation: Current high-throughput screening for molecular glues and proximity-inducing compounds often requires parallel, separate assays of the target protein alone and in complex with its partner, which is resource-intensive and can miss compounds that only stabilize the interaction without directly inhibiting the target. This limits the discovery of novel chemical matter that modulates protein-protein interactions (PPIs), a major frontier in chemical biology and therapeutics, particularly for undruggable targets. A unified assay that captures the desired phenotype—enhanced complex formation—in a single well would dramatically increase throughput and hit relevance.
proposals.hypothesis: A bimolecular fluorescence complementation (BiFC) assay, where the target protein and its partner are fused to complementary fragments of a fluorescent protein, will enable the direct, single-well identification of small molecules that stabilize their interaction, without requiring a separate counter-screen against the isolated target.
proposals.method: We will engineer a suite of HEK293T cell lines stably expressing pairs of proteins of interest (e.g., p53 and MDM2, or other disease-relevant E3 ligase-substrate pairs) fused to non-fluorescent fragments of Venus fluorescent protein. Compound libraries, including Schreiber's diversity-oriented synthesis (DOS) collections, will be screened in a single-addition format. Fluorescence signal, reconstituted only upon stable target-partner proximity, will be quantified via high-content imaging. Hits will be validated using established orthogonal methods, including cellular thermal shift assays (CETSA) for target engagement and immunoprecipitation followed by mass spectrometry to confirm enhanced complex formation and specificity.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a robust, generalizable screening platform that streamlines the discovery of molecular glues and ternary complex inducers, directly addressing a key bottleneck in chemical genetics and targeted protein degradation. It will provide the field with a practical tool to identify chemical probes for challenging PPIs, potentially unlocking new therapeutic modalities for cancers and other diseases driven by dysregulated protein interactions.
proposals.novelty: While BiFC is an established PPI detection tool, its application as a primary, single-well high-throughput screening format specifically designed to bypass the need for parallel target-alone counterscreens is novel. This directly inverts the standard screening paradigm for molecular glues, focusing the assay readout exclusively on the desired functional outcome—complex stabilization.

proposals.title: Cooperative Ternary Complex Formation via a Two-Molecule Proximity Inducing System
proposals.motivation: Targeted protein degradation and proximity-induced pharmacology, exemplified by PROTACs and molecular glues, rely on a single bifunctional molecule to recruit an E3 ligase to a target protein. This paradigm imposes significant constraints on molecular design, as both binding moieties must be linked into a single, cell-permeable entity. A system using two separate, cooperating molecules to recruit a third effector protein would dramatically expand the chemical space for proximity-inducing therapeutics and enable the targeting of proteins currently considered 'undruggable' due to the lack of suitable bifunctional ligands.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that two separate small molecules, each binding distinct sites on a target protein, can cooperatively create a composite interface with high affinity and specificity for a third, recruited protein (e.g., an E3 ubiquitin ligase), thereby inducing its proximity and subsequent post-translational modification of the target.
proposals.method: We will employ a structure-guided approach using X-ray crystallography and cryo-EM to identify pairs of allosteric and orthosteric pockets on model target proteins (e.g., mutant p53, BRD4). Parallel synthetic chemistry will generate libraries of potential 'cooperative binders' based on fragments identified from the Schreiber lab's diversity-oriented synthesis (DOS) collections. High-throughput screening using fluorescence polarization and AlphaScreen will identify molecule pairs that synergistically enhance recruitment of a labeled E3 ligase domain (e.g., VHL). Positive hits will be optimized using structure-activity relationship (SAR) studies and evaluated in cellular models for target ubiquitination and degradation.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new conceptual and practical framework for proximity-inducing pharmacology, moving beyond the single-molecule recruiter paradigm. It will deliver first-in-class chemical tools that demonstrate cooperative ternary complex formation and validate a novel strategy for inducing protein degradation or other post-translational modifications with enhanced specificity and reduced off-target effects.
proposals.novelty: While heterobifunctional molecules and molecular glues are established, the proposed system is novel in its explicit use of two separate, non-covalently linked small molecules that act cooperatively, not independently, to create a de novo protein-protein interaction surface for a third party effector, a mechanism distinct from traditional fragment-based drug discovery or dual-pharmacology approaches.

proposals.title: Systematic Discovery of Small-Molecule Inducers of Non-Degradative Protein-Proximity for Signaling Modulation and Stabilization
proposals.motivation: Targeted protein degradation via PROTACs and molecular glues has revolutionized chemical biology and therapeutics, but this focuses almost exclusively on inducing ubiquitination and proteasomal destruction. Many critical cellular processes, such as signal transduction, complex assembly, and protein stabilization, are governed by proximity without degradation. A systematic method to discover small molecules that induce these non-degradative proximity effects is lacking, limiting our ability to pharmacologically modulate a vast array of protein functions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by adapting high-throughput screening platforms used for degraders, but employing orthogonal readouts sensitive to protein association, stabilization, or pathway activation, we can identify novel small-molecule inducers of non-degradative proximity with specific functional outcomes.
proposals.method: We will adapt a cell-based, high-content screening platform using a NanoLuc Binary Technology (NanoBiT) complementation reporter system. Target proteins of interest (e.g., p53, specific signaling nodes) will be tagged with either LgBiT or SmBiT fragments. A primary screen will identify compounds that induce luminescence complementation, indicating proximity. Hits will be triaged in secondary assays: (1) monitoring target protein abundance via immunoblotting to exclude degraders, (2) assessing downstream pathway activation via phospho-specific antibodies or transcriptional reporters, and (3) using AlphaFold2 (pre-2026 version) to model potential ternary complex interfaces for prioritized hits, guiding mechanistic studies and analog synthesis.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a generalizable screening paradigm for 'non-degradative molecular glues,' expanding the pharmacological toolkit beyond degradation. It will deliver first-in-class chemical probes that stabilize proteins like p53 or rewire signaling pathways through induced proximity, validating a new therapeutic modality. The platform will provide a public resource for interrogating protein-protein interaction networks functionally.
proposals.novelty: While screens for degraders are established, deliberately screening for and validating proximity-inducing molecules that *avoid* degradation to instead activate or stabilize proteins is a distinct and underexplored direction. This shifts the paradigm from eliminating proteins to controlling their native functions through engineered association.

proposals.title: Phage Display of Bifunctional Peptide Ligands for Targeted Intracellular Protein-Proximity Induction
proposals.motivation: Targeted protein degradation and proximity-induced pharmacology, exemplified by PROTACs and molecular glues, have revolutionized therapeutic intervention but are largely limited to small molecules. Peptides offer unique potential for targeting challenging protein interfaces and achieving high specificity, yet a generalizable discovery platform for peptide-based proximity inducers is lacking. Phage display, a proven method for high-throughput peptide discovery against protein targets, remains underexploited for generating bifunctional molecules that co-opt cellular ubiquitination or other effector machinery.
proposals.hypothesis: Phage-displayed combinatorial peptide libraries can be screened to identify bifunctional peptide ligands that simultaneously bind a target protein of interest and an E3 ubiquitin ligase (or other effector protein), thereby inducing targeted intracellular proximity and subsequent ubiquitination or functional modulation.
proposals.method: We will engineer M13 phage libraries displaying constrained cyclic peptides (e.g., via disulfide bridges) fused to a flexible linker and a known high-affinity peptide ligand for a selected E3 ligase, such as the VHL ligand. These bifunctional libraries will be panned against purified, immobilized target proteins (e.g., mutant p53, oncogenic transcription factors) under stringent conditions. Positive clones will be sequenced, and hits will be synthesized as cell-permeable peptides using stapling or arginine grafting. Functional validation will employ cellular NanoBRET assays to measure target-E3 proximity, immunoblotting for target ubiquitination and degradation, and phenotypic assays in relevant cancer cell lines.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a generalizable platform for the discovery of peptide-based proximity inducers, expanding the druggable space beyond traditional small molecules. It will deliver first-in-class peptide leads for 'undruggable' targets and provide fundamental insights into the design rules for effective peptide-mediated ternary complex formation and intracellular effector recruitment.
proposals.novelty: While phage display is mature for monovalent binders, its application to directly select for bifunctional, proximity-inducing peptides is novel. This approach merges the high-specificity potential of peptide libraries with the powerful mechanistic paradigm of proximity-inducing compounds central to Schreiber's recent work on molecular glues and targeted degradation.

proposals.title: DEL-Based Discovery of Competitive Proximity Disruptors for therapeutically relevant protein-protein interactions
proposals.motivation: The field of targeted protein degradation and molecular glues has focused overwhelmingly on inducing proximity to trigger degradation or neomorphic signaling. However, many disease-associated protein complexes, such as oncogenic transcription factor assemblies or pathological viral-host protein interactions, are drivers of pathology and require disruption, not stabilization. Current small-molecule discovery platforms are poorly suited for identifying competitive inhibitors of large, shallow protein-protein interfaces (PPIs).
proposals.hypothesis: DNA-encoded library (DEL) technology, when coupled with a competitive binding screen against a pre-formed, disease-relevant protein complex, can efficiently identify small molecules that act as proximity disruptors by competitively binding to one partner and preventing complex assembly.
proposals.method: We will employ a model system, such as the p53-MDM2/MDMX complex in TP53 wild-type cancers, where disrupting the interaction stabilizes p53. A biotinylated, pre-formed p53-MDM2 complex will be immobilized on streptavidin beads. A large, diverse DEL (containing >1 billion compounds) will be screened against this target in a competitive format, where binders to free MDM2 or p53 are deselected in prior steps. Hits that elute the complex will be identified via DNA sequencing and PCR amplification. Validated disruptors will be characterized using AlphaScreen, SPR, and cellular assays for p53 stabilization and transcriptional activation.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a generalizable DEL screening paradigm for discovering proximity-disrupting small molecules, expanding the chemical toolbox beyond degraders and glues. It will deliver first-in-class chemical probes for disrupting a therapeutically significant PPI, validating the p53-MDM2 interface as a 'disruption' window. The methodology will provide a blueprint for targeting other non-enzymatic, complex-dependent oncogenic drivers.
proposals.novelty: While DELs are used for target-based screening, applying them in a competitive format against a pre-formed multi-protein complex to specifically find disruptors is a novel inversion of the prevailing 'induced proximity' paradigm. This approach directly addresses the historical challenge of finding small-molecule inhibitors of PPIs by leveraging the massive library size and selection power of DELs.

proposals.title: One-Pot, Multiplexed DNA-Encoded Library Screening for Pan-E3 Ligase Effector Engagers
proposals.motivation: Targeted protein degradation via molecular glues and PROTACs has emerged as a transformative therapeutic strategy, but its scope is limited by the small number of E3 ligases currently exploited. Most efforts focus on single ligase-target pairs, failing to capture the complex polypharmacology and redundancy inherent in the ubiquitin-proteasome system. A method to simultaneously discover ligands for multiple E3 ligases and their effectors would dramatically accelerate the mapping of therapeutically relevant degradation pathways.
proposals.hypothesis: A multiplexed DNA-encoded library (DEL) screen conducted in a single pot against a panel of purified, barcoded E3 ligases and target proteins will yield selective, high-affinity small molecules capable of inducing proximity between specific E3-effector pairs, including novel ligases not previously amenable to chemical recruitment.
proposals.method: We will design and synthesize a DEL biased toward known molecular glue chemotypes and E3 ligase binding motifs. A panel of 10-15 recombinant, His-tagged human E3 ligases (e.g., CRBN, VHL, IAPs, DCAFs) and 5-8 target proteins of interest (e.g., transcription factors, undrugged enzymes) will be individually conjugated to unique DNA barcodes. These barcoded proteins will be pooled and incubated with the DEL in a single screening pot. After selection, high-throughput sequencing and bioinformatic deconvolution (using established DEL analysis pipelines like SICLOPPS or custom alignment algorithms) will identify DNA codes enriched for specific E3-target co-binding events, pinpointing candidate bifunctional or glue-like compounds.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first platform for the parallel discovery of ligands engaging multiple E3 ligases, generating a rich dataset of chemical starting points for novel degraders. It will provide fundamental insights into the ligandability of diverse E3 families and potentially reveal new, tissue-specific degradation axes for therapeutic intervention in cancer and other diseases.
proposals.novelty: While DELs have been used to screen single targets, applying multiplexed, one-pot screening to a complex mixture of E3 ligases and effector proteins to directly identify polypharmacological glue compounds is a novel and untested strategy that moves beyond binary target-based discovery.

proposals.title: Epitranscriptomic Reactivation of p53 via NSUN2 Inhibition as a Therapeutic Strategy in Fibrosis
proposals.motivation: Wild-type p53 has critical cytoprotective functions beyond tumor suppression, including anti-fibrotic and metabolic regulation. In diseases like idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis (IPF), p53 is often dysfunctional despite high expression, contributing to pathology. Pre-2026 work shows the RNA methyltransferase NSUN2 can regulate mRNA stability and translation via m5C modification, but its role in disabling p53 in non-cancerous disease is unexplored.
proposals.hypothesis: NSUN2-mediated methylation of p53 mRNA (or transcripts of key co-factors like MDM2) represses p53 protein function in fibrotic tissues, and targeted inhibition of NSUN2 will restore p53's native anti-fibrotic activity without inducing genomic instability.
proposals.method: We will employ Schreiber lab expertise in molecular glues and targeted protein degradation. First, we will use RNA immunoprecipitation sequencing (MeRIP-seq) and LC-MS/MS in primary human fibroblasts to map NSUN2 binding and m5C marks on the p53 transcriptome. Second, we will screen a library of known NSUN2-inhibitory small molecules and design bifunctional degraders (PROTACs) using a cereblon-binding ligand linked to an NSUN2 inhibitor (e.g., based on the sinefungin scaffold) to achieve specific intracellular degradation. Functional rescue will be assayed by RNA-seq, collagen deposition, and senescence markers in TGF-β-stimulated and patient-derived fibrotic cells.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel epitranscriptomic mechanism of p53 dysfunction in fibrosis and provide a first-in-class chemical strategy to reactivate wild-type p53 in a non-oncological context. It will deliver a potent, selective NSUN2 degrader as a chemical probe to dissect this pathway and a potential therapeutic lead for fibrotic diseases.
proposals.novelty: The proposal re-purposes an epitranscriptomic enzyme, typically studied in cancer, as a target to restore—not inhibit—a tumor suppressor's physiological function in a non-cancer disease, leveraging chemical degradation for precise targeting.

proposals.title: Structure-Activity-Relationship-Guided Optimization of p53-Stabilizing Molecular Glues for Improved Pharmacokinetics and Tumor Penetration
proposals.motivation: Targeted protein degradation via proximity-inducing small molecules (molecular glues) offers a powerful therapeutic strategy for cancers with TP53 mutations by restoring p53 tumor suppressor function. However, the initial lead compounds often possess suboptimal drug-like properties, including poor solubility, metabolic instability, and limited tissue penetration, which hinder their in vivo efficacy and clinical translation. Optimizing these pharmacokinetic parameters is critical to realizing the therapeutic potential of p53-stabilizing molecular glues.
proposals.hypothesis: Systematic, iterative medicinal chemistry guided by in vitro ADME (Absorption, Distribution, Metabolism, Excretion) profiling and predictive modeling will yield p53-stabilizing molecular glue derivatives with improved solubility, metabolic stability, and tumor penetration while retaining high target engagement and degradation potency.
proposals.method: We will employ a multi-parameter optimization strategy. First, we will synthesize focused libraries of analogs based on the published p53-stabilizing glue scaffold, systematically modifying regions predicted to influence logP, solubility, and metabolic soft spots. Second, compounds will be screened in parallel for p53 degradation (cell-based NanoBRET), cytotoxicity in TP53 mutant cell lines, and key ADME properties (microsomal stability, Caco-2 permeability, plasma protein binding). Third, we will utilize in silico tools (e.g., Schrödinger's QikProp) to model physicochemical properties and develop predictive QSAR models for stability and permeability. Top candidates will be evaluated for pharmacokinetics in mice and tumor penetration using orthotopic xenograft models with quantitative LC-MS/MS analysis of tumor homogenates.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver novel, in vivo-efficacious lead compounds that validate p53 stabilization via molecular glues as a viable therapeutic strategy. It will establish a generalizable medicinal chemistry roadmap for optimizing the pharmacokinetic profile of proximity-inducing compounds, a common bottleneck in the field. The resulting optimized glue will serve as a critical tool for probing p53 biology in vivo and advancing toward clinical development.
proposals.novelty: While molecular glue discovery is advancing, systematic PK/PD-driven optimization of a glue for an intracellular tumor suppressor target like p53 is unexplored. This project uniquely integrates predictive ADME modeling with functional degradation assays to guide the design of glues with enhanced drug-like properties, moving beyond simple potency optimization.

proposals.title: Targeted Degradation of Mutant p53 via Heterobifunctional PROTACs: A Novel Therapeutic Strategy for TP53-Mutant Cancers
proposals.motivation: Mutant p53 is a highly prevalent oncoprotein, present in over half of all human cancers, and its accumulation often drives tumor progression and chemoresistance. Unlike loss-of-function mutations, many p53 mutants are not druggable by conventional inhibitors, creating a critical unmet need. Recent work, including from our lab, has shown that p53 protein abundance itself is a therapeutic window, suggesting that degrading the mutant protein could be a viable strategy.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that heterobifunctional PROTAC (PROteolysis TArgeting Chimera) molecules can be designed to selectively bind mutant p53 and recruit an E3 ubiquitin ligase, leading to the ubiquitination and proteasomal degradation of mutant p53 and a consequent anti-proliferative effect in cancer cells.
proposals.method: We will leverage our expertise in molecular glues and bifunctional compound synthesis to design PROTACs linking a mutant p53-binding ligand (e.g., derived from known stabilizers or fragment-based screening hits) to ligands for specific E3 ligases (e.g., VHL, CRBN, or MDM2). Compounds will be synthesized using modular, diversity-oriented chemistry. Degradation will be assessed in isogenic and TP53-mutant cancer cell lines via immunoblotting, cycloheximide chase, and global proteomics (TMT-MS). Anti-proliferative effects will be measured in 2D and 3D cultures, with mechanistic validation using E3 ligase knockout cells and proteasome inhibitors.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a first-in-class chemical strategy for the direct degradation of mutant p53, a major oncogenic driver. It will provide a novel set of chemical probes to study mutant p53 biology and validate its degradation as a therapeutic modality. Success would lay the groundwork for a new class of therapeutics for a broad spectrum of cancers harboring TP53 mutations.
proposals.novelty: While PROTACs have been made for many targets, a dedicated campaign to degrade mutant p53—specifically exploiting its altered conformation and stability—is novel. This approach moves beyond stabilizing or inhibiting mutant p53 to its complete elimination, a distinct mechanism not yet fully explored pre-2026.

proposals.title: Fragment-Based Assembly of Molecular Glues for Mutant p53: A Materials Science-Inspired Approach to Protein Surface Targeting
proposals.motivation: Mutant p53 proteins, which are prevalent in over 50% of human cancers, often lose tumor-suppressor function but can acquire oncogenic gain-of-function properties. Restoring wild-type function or degrading mutant p53 via targeted protein degradation represents a major therapeutic opportunity, yet these proteins are historically considered 'undruggable' due to a lack of deep, well-defined binding pockets. Current proximity-inducing molecules for p53 are limited, highlighting a need for novel strategies to generate specific binders to its complex surface topology.
proposals.hypothesis: By adapting the fragment assembly paradigm from Metal-Organic Framework (MOF) discovery, where optimal structures emerge from screening and linking fundamental building blocks, we hypothesize that screening ultra-small molecular fragments against mutant p53 will reveal adjacent, weak-binding motifs that can be rationally linked to create high-affinity, composite-binding 'molecular glues' or stabilizers.
proposals.method: We will employ a tiered fragment-based drug discovery (FBDD) pipeline. First, a diverse library of 1000+ ultra-low molecular weight (<150 Da) fragments will be screened against purified, common 'hotspot' mutant p53 (e.g., R175H, R273H) using surface plasmon resonance (SPR) and protein-observed NMR to identify weak binders (Kd >100 µM). Co-crystal structures of fragment-protein complexes will be solved to map binding epitopes. Inspired by MOF linker-node assembly, fragments binding to proximal but distinct surface sites will be computationally linked using structure-based design with programs like Schrödinger's CovalentDock and Prime, followed by chemical synthesis and iterative optimization of linker chemistry to yield bivalent compounds with enhanced affinity and selectivity.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel chemical strategy and potential lead compounds for targeting mutant p53, directly extending the Schreiber lab's work on proximity-inducing molecules. It will establish a proof-of-concept for a materials-science-inspired 'fragment assembly' methodology in chemical biology, potentially applicable to other challenging protein targets. The resulting compounds will serve as valuable chemical probes to study mutant p53 biology and validate its therapeutic window.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit conceptual translation of the MOF discovery framework—systematic screening and rational assembly of fundamental building blocks—to the problem of constructing a composite binding site on a protein surface, moving beyond traditional FBDD for single pockets to create multi-epitope binders de novo.

proposals.title: A Fragment-Based DNA-Encoded Library Platform for Bivalent Disruption of the Mutant p53-NSUN2 Oncogenic Axis
proposals.motivation: Gain-of-function (GOF) mutant p53 proteins drive tumorigenesis through aberrant protein-protein interactions (PPIs), while NSUN2-mediated RNA methylation promotes oncogenic translation, creating a linked, undrugged vulnerability. Current small-molecule approaches struggle to target the shallow, dynamic interfaces of these proteins. A method to simultaneously discover minimal binders and rationally assemble them into PPI disruptors is urgently needed.
proposals.hypothesis: A single, fragment-based DNA-encoded library (DEL), screened against a network of GOF mutant p53 and NSUN2 proteins, will yield specific fragment pairs that, when linked via their DNA tags, generate bivalent molecules capable of selectively disrupting the mutant p53-NSUN2 interactome with higher potency and specificity than monovalent fragments.
proposals.method: We will construct a fragment-based DEL (fDEL) using a split-and-pool approach, encoding ~100,000 minimal, 3D-diverse fragments. This library will be screened against an immobilized protein network of common GOF p53 mutants (R175H, R273H) and NSUN2. Hit identification will use high-throughput sequencing and the 'TBL' (Target-Bound Library) analysis pipeline to deconvolve binding fragments. Crucially, the DNA barcodes of co-enriched fragment pairs will inform the design of linked, bivalent compounds via on-DNA synthesis, creating potential molecular glues or direct inhibitors.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel chemical proteomics platform that merges fragment screening with DEL-enabled bivalent assembly. It will produce first-in-class chemical probes targeting the mutant p53-NSUN2 axis, validating this network as a therapeutic vulnerability. The platform itself is a generalizable strategy for targeting other oncogenic PPIs.
proposals.novelty: The fusion of fragment-based principles with DEL technology for the parallel, network-level screening and immediate in-library assembly of bivalent disruptors is a novel strategy, distinct from conventional target-by-target DEL screening or structure-based fragment linking.

proposals.title: Functional Clustering of p53 Missense Mutants Reveals Cluster-Specific Chemical Vulnerabilities via Chemical Genomics
proposals.motivation: TP53 missense mutations are the most common genetic alterations in human cancer, but they are highly heterogeneous, complicating targeted therapy. While recent work, including from our lab, shows p53 protein abundance is a therapeutic window, most strategies treat all mutants as a single class. A deeper functional classification is needed to exploit distinct, mutation-specific neomorphic activities for precision oncology.
proposals.hypothesis: Missense p53 mutants can be classified into discrete functional clusters based on protein-protein interaction networks and transcriptional outputs, and each cluster possesses unique chemical vulnerabilities that can be revealed by high-throughput screening against an isogenic mutant panel.
proposals.method: We will first establish a panel of isogenic cell lines expressing representative p53 missense mutants (e.g., R175H, R248W, R273H). For functional clustering, we will perform multiplexed co-immunoprecipitation-mass spectrometry (using TMT labeling) to define mutant-specific interactomes and employ a suite of p53 transcriptional reporter assays. Clustering will be performed using dimensionality reduction (UMAP) and hierarchical clustering. In parallel, we will screen a diverse library of small molecules, including bifunctional degraders (PROTACs) and molecular glues, against the panel. Integration of chemical sensitivity profiles with functional clusters will identify cluster-specific lethal compounds.
proposals.contribution: This work will move the field beyond a binary mutant/wild-type view of p53 by establishing a functional taxonomy of missense mutants. It will deliver novel chemical starting points (small molecules or degraders) that selectively target specific mutant p53 functionalities, providing a blueprint for patient stratification based on mutant phenotype rather than genotype alone.
proposals.novelty: This integrates deep functional proteomic profiling directly with chemical genomics in an isogenic system to discover vulnerabilities, moving from correlative genotype-to-drug maps to a mechanistic, function-to-drug discovery paradigm for p53 mutants.

proposals.title: Discovery of Mutant p53 Abundance-Dependent Synthetic Lethalities via Chemical Genomics and Targeted Protein Degradation
proposals.motivation: TP53 is the most frequently mutated gene in human cancer, but directly targeting mutant p53 proteins has proven challenging. Recent work, including from our lab, demonstrates that high mutant p53 protein abundance itself can be a therapeutic vulnerability, as it can be targeted for degradation by proximity-inducing molecules. This creates a novel opportunity to identify payloads that are selectively toxic in cells with high mutant p53 burden, offering a new therapeutic paradigm for p53-mutant cancers.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a chemical genomics screen will reveal small-molecule payloads that exhibit synthetic lethality specifically in the context of high mutant p53 protein abundance, and that these payloads can be rationally linked to mutant p53 degraders to create mutant-specific, bifunctional therapeutics.
proposals.method: We will employ a chemical genomics approach using a diverse library of bioactive small molecules and targeted protein degraders (PROTACs/molecular glues). Isogenic cell line pairs, differing only in TP53 mutation status and engineered for inducible mutant p53 expression, will be screened in high-throughput format. Viability data will be analyzed using the GEM (Gene Expression Modeling) or PRISM algorithms to identify compounds whose lethality correlates strongly with mutant p53 abundance. Top hits will be validated and their mechanisms probed via CRISPR-based genetic screens and proteomic profiling.
proposals.contribution: This work will identify novel, mutant p53 context-dependent vulnerabilities, expanding the therapeutic landscape for TP53-mutant cancers. It will provide a generalizable framework for discovering payloads that act synergistically with targeted degradation strategies, moving beyond simple degradation to induce synthetic lethality. The resulting bifunctional compound concepts could serve as leads for a new class of precision oncology therapeutics.
proposals.novelty: While mutant p53 degraders exist, this proposal uniquely seeks to exploit the resultant high-abundance state as a conditional synthetic lethal screen trigger, moving from target degradation to targeted cell killing. It integrates chemical genomics with the emerging paradigm of proximity-induced pharmacology in a novel, context-specific manner.

proposals.title: Catalytic Molecular Glues for Amplified In Vivo Imaging of Mutant p53-Positive Tumors
proposals.motivation: Mutant p53 proteins are highly abundant in many cancers and represent a compelling, tumor-specific diagnostic target. However, current imaging modalities lack the sensitivity to detect low-density or micrometastatic lesions. A method that converts mutant p53 abundance into an amplified, localized signal could enable earlier detection and more precise surgical guidance.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that mutant p53 proteins can function as essential catalytic cofactors to activate a tethered, engineered enzyme, thereby triggering localized, amplified signal generation specifically within tumor cells.
proposals.method: We will design modular probes consisting of a mutant p53-binding small molecule (e.g., a derivative of known reactivators or stabilizers) covalently linked to an engineered, catalytically inactive DNAzyme or split enzyme scaffold. This scaffold will be immobilized on a nanoparticle carrier decorated with multiple quenched fluorogenic substrates. Binding of the probe to mutant p53 will induce a conformational change or proximity-driven reassembly, restoring enzyme activity. The activated enzyme will then catalytically cleave the quenched substrates on the same particle, releasing a burst of fluorescence signal localized to the tumor cell.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new paradigm for molecular imaging where a disease-specific protein acts not just as a passive target but as a catalytic trigger for signal amplification. It will deliver a prototype probe with significantly enhanced tumor-to-background ratio for mutant p53-positive cancers, directly addressing a major limitation in oncologic imaging.
proposals.novelty: This approach is novel in merging the concepts of proximity-inducing molecular glues and catalytic molecular beacons from environmental sensing into a diagnostic tool for oncology, creating a protein-activated, self-amplifying imaging system.

proposals.title: Conditional Intracellular Assembly of a Multi-Mechanistic Toxin via Split-Protein Complementation Guided by Mutant p53
proposals.motivation: Mutant p53 is a highly prevalent, tumor-specific antigen across many cancers, yet it has remained a challenging direct drug target. Current small-molecule approaches, including proteolysis-targeting chimeras (PROTACs), often struggle with selectivity and on-target, off-tumor toxicity. A method to deliver a potent, multi-mechanistic cytotoxic payload exclusively to mutant p53-harboring cells would address a major unmet need in oncology.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a two-component system, where one fragment binds mutant p53 and the other is cell-penetrant, will enable intracellular, tumor-specific complementation of a split toxin, leading to selective cancer cell killing with minimal systemic toxicity.
proposals.method: We will engineer a targeting module consisting of a high-affinity mutant p53-binding protein (e.g., a designed ankyrin repeat protein or antibody fragment) fused to fragment A of a split, engineered bacterial toxin (e.g., a modified Pseudomonas exotoxin A). The second module will be a cell-penetrant peptide or small molecule conjugate carrying fragment B. We will employ split-protein complementation systems (e.g., split inteins or engineered fragment pairs) that spontaneously reassemble upon co-localization in the reducing environment of the cytosol. The reassembled toxin will be designed to inhibit protein synthesis and disrupt mitochondrial function.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel therapeutic modality that merges the specificity of biologic targeting with the potency of small-molecule toxins. It will provide a proof-of-concept for conditional intracellular assembly as a general strategy to achieve spatial control over highly cytotoxic agents, directly addressing the selectivity problem in targeted cancer therapy.
proposals.novelty: While split-toxins and proximity-inducing molecules exist, this approach uniquely combines mutant p53 targeting with a cell-penetrant co-precursor to drive intracellular toxin assembly, creating a multi-layered specificity gate not achieved by single-agent PROTACs or antibody-drug conjugates.

ideas.title: Fragment-based DNA-encoded library screening for RNA methyltransferase-p53 interactome disruptors
ideas.description: This research direction fuses fragment-based principles with DNA-encoded library (DEL) technology to develop a novel chemical proteomics platform. The goal is to screen a single, diverse fragment-based DEL not just against individual targets, but against a protein network comprising gain-of-function mutant p53 isoforms and NSUN family RNA methyltransferases (like NSUN2), which are implicated in a shared oncogenic axis. The synergy enables the parallel identification of minimal fragment binders to multiple, traditionally 'undruggable' surfaces. Crucially, the DNA tag allows for the immediate in-library assembly of linked fragments, generating first-in-class bivalent molecules designed to either directly inhibit NSUN2 or selectively disrupt specific mutant p53/NSUN protein-protein interactions, a strategy neither parent approach could efficiently achieve alone.

ideas.title: PROTAC-Mediated NSUN2 Degradation to Modulate tRNA m5C and Target Mutant p53 in Cancer
ideas.description: This project designs heterobifunctional PROTAC molecules to specifically degrade the tRNA methyltransferase NSUN2, thereby inducing a loss of m5C modifications on specific tRNA species. We will investigate the hypothesis that NSUN2 degradation creates a unique cellular stress state—through disrupted translation of key oncogenic transcripts—that selectively sensitizes cancer cells harboring mutant p53. The synergy lies in using PROTACs not just as a direct degradation tool, but as a precise perturbation to study the tRNA epitranscriptome, and to test if mutant p53 cells become uniquely dependent on NSUN2 function for survival, revealing a novel combined therapeutic vulnerability.

ideas.title: Adapting Fragment-Based Drug Discovery from Kinase Research for NSUN Family ABPP
ideas.description: The original idea to use cysteine-directed ABPP to discover covalent inhibitors for NSUN1, NSUN3, NSUN4, NSUN5, or NSUN7 faces the challenge of achieving selectivity among closely related family members. Drawing an analogy from kinase drug discovery, where the analogous problem of targeting a conserved active site across a large protein family was solved using fragment-based drug discovery (FBDD). In that field, researchers screened small, low-molecular-weight fragments against the conserved ATP-binding site and then chemically elaborated hits to gain selectivity. Adapting this solution, the research idea is to first perform a cysteine-directed ABPP screen using a minimal library of weakly reactive electrophilic fragments against a panel of NSUN proteins. Initial non-selective hits that engage the target cysteine residues can then be used as starting points for structure-guided chemical elaboration. By adding chemical features that interact with unique residues in the local environment of the cysteine in each NSUN protein (e.g., NSUN3 vs. NSUN5), this approach aims to generate highly selective covalent inhibitors and probes for individual NSUN family members, overcoming the limitation of broad cysteine-reactive library screens.

ideas.title: Covalent Chemical Genomics for Mutant p53-Specific Lethality via NSUN2 Inhibition
ideas.description: This research direction integrates the chemical probe question of Idea A with the phenotypic screening strategy of Idea B. We will perform a chemical genomics screen using isogenic cell lines with high mutant p53 abundance, testing matched pairs of covalent and reversible NSUN2 inhibitors. The core hypothesis is that only the covalent, and therefore sustained, inhibition of NSUN2 will create a unique RNA methylation landscape that is synthetically lethal in the mutant p53 context, while reversible inhibition will not. This approach directly tests whether covalent engagement is functionally necessary for achieving mutant genotype-selective cell death, moving beyond simple binding selectivity to therapeutic-window selectivity.

ideas.title: Conditional intracellular assembly of a multi-mechanistic toxin via split-protein complementation guided by mutant p53
ideas.description: This redesigned approach uses two separate, non-toxic biologic precursors. One component is a targeting module that binds mutant p53 and carries a split-toxin fragment A. The second is a cell-penetrant module carrying split-toxin fragment B. Only upon co-localization inside a mutant p53-expressing cancer cell do fragments A and B undergo spontaneous complementation to form the active, multi-mechanistic toxin. This spatial and conditional control ensures systemic administration of inert precursors, drastically reducing off-target toxicity while delivering a potent, combined payload specifically to the tumor.

ideas.title: Mapping Cryptic Pockets in NSUN2 via Fragment-Based Allosteric Screening
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that the catalytic cysteine is the only druggable site in NSUN2 by drawing an analogy from the field of structural biology and allosteric drug discovery, specifically the successful targeting of proteins like KRAS. The analogous solution involved using fragment-based screening and advanced structural methods to discover and exploit cryptic, allosteric pockets that were not evident in apo-protein structures. Adapted to NSUN2, the research idea is to systematically employ fragment-based lead discovery (FBLD) coupled with high-throughput structural biology (e.g., X-ray crystallography or cryo-EM) to screen for small molecules that bind outside the catalytic site. Identifying and characterizing such fragment-bound complexes would reveal novel, potentially allosteric binding pockets, providing a foundation for developing inhibitors that modulate NSUN2 function through a mechanism distinct from direct catalytic cysteine blockade.

ideas.title: Functional-cluster-guided chemical genomics for p53 mutation-specific vulnerabilities
ideas.description: This approach first classifies diverse p53 missense mutations into discrete functional clusters (e.g., aggregation-prone, altered DNA-contact, constitutive binder) using multiplexed co-immunoprecipitation-mass spectrometry and reporter assays. In parallel, a high-throughput chemical screen is performed against an isogenic cell line panel engineered to express representative mutants from each cluster. By integrating the chemical sensitivity data with the deep functional profiling, the system identifies compound classes whose lethality is linked to the specific neomorphic biology of a mutant cluster, not just its presence. This yields targeted payload strategies applicable to broad groups of patients sharing a common mutant p53 functional phenotype.

ideas.title: Employ fragment-based drug discovery for new binders, inspired by fragment-based lead discovery in materials science
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from materials science, specifically Metal-Organic Framework (MOF) design, where libraries of molecular 'linker' and 'node' fragments are screened to discover optimal combinations for self-assembly into functional structures. The analogous solution involves high-throughput screening of fragment libraries to identify binding motifs. Adapted to drug discovery for mutant p53, the method involves first screening ultra-small molecular fragments to find weak binders to various surface pockets on the mutant protein. Following the MOF assembly paradigm, these validated fragment 'hits' binding to adjacent sites are then rationally linked or merged using structure-based design to create a novel, higher-affinity small molecule that targets a composite surface site, thereby constructing a drug lead from fundamental binding units.

ideas.title: Investigate tRNA m5C loss by mapping the 'epitranscriptomic network collapse' using a systems biology approach
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from ecology and network science, where the collapse of a system following the removal of a keystone element is studied by mapping interaction networks and simulating cascading failures. The analogous problem is understanding the consequences of a keystone species' removal. Here, NSUN2 (the writer of tRNA m5C) is the 'keystone' enzyme. The adapted research idea is to move beyond correlative phenotypes and instead map the 'epitranscriptomic translation network' dependent on m5C-modified tRNAs. By using techniques like Ribo-seq and quantitative proteomics upon NSUN2 inhibition in different cancer cell types, combined with computational modeling, the study will identify which mRNA translation events are most vulnerable, predict the cascade of proteomic changes, and link specific network fragilities to downstream cellular consequences like differentiation defects or metabolic reprogramming.

ideas.title: Molecular Beacon Probes for Mutant p53 Imaging via Catalytic Signal Amplification
ideas.description: This idea adapts a catalytic molecular beacon strategy from environmental sensing to develop highly sensitive tumor imaging agents. In the analogous field, DNAzymes use trace contaminants as catalytic cofactors to amplify a detection signal. Here, we propose to design diagnostic probes where the abundant mutant p53 protein in tumors serves as the essential cofactor to activate a tethered enzyme (e.g., a DNAzyme or engineered peptide enzyme). Upon binding, the activated enzyme catalytically cleaves multiple quenched fluorophores attached to the same nanoparticle carrier, leading to a localized signal explosion. This approach uses mutant p53 abundance not just for targeting but as a catalytic trigger, concentrating the imaging agent's signal specifically within tumor tissue for enhanced detection and imaging.
Use activity-based protein profiling with ubiquitin-vinyl sulfone probes to identify enzymes affected by ubiquitin monomer accumulation.

ideas.title: Empirically determining the therapeutic direction of NSUN2-tRNA interaction modulation using a tunable dimerization system.
ideas.description: This redesigned approach addresses the core flaw of assuming a therapeutic direction by creating a system to empirically test it. A chemical-dimerizer platform is used to engineer cells where the interaction strength between NSUN2 and tRNA can be precisely and reversibly tuned from inhibition to stabilization. This system is then deployed in high-content phenotypic screens using disease-relevant cellular models (e.g., for cancer or neurological disorders) to map the functional consequences of interaction modulation on disease hallmarks. The outcome is a data-driven, therapeutic hypothesis—identifying whether a stabilizer or an inhibitor of the NSUN2-tRNA interaction holds genuine therapeutic potential for a specific disease context.

ideas.title: Targeting NSUN2 to reactivate dysfunctional wild-type p53 in non-cancer pathologies
ideas.description: This research direction proposes that the RNA methyltransferase NSUN2 can be a pathological driver in specific non-cancer contexts by functionally inactivating wild-type p53 through epitranscriptomic mechanisms, such as methylating p53 mRNA or transcripts of key co-factors. Inhibiting NSUN2 in these defined settings—potentially including fibrotic diseases, certain neurodegenerative conditions, or severe metabolic syndromes—would aim to restore p53's native cytoprotective, anti-fibrotic, or metabolic regulatory functions without the cancer-centric risk of genomic instability. This exploits the 'Achilles heel' of dysfunctional but highly expressed wild-type p53, making NSUN2 inhibition relevant and potentially safe outside of oncology. The core novelty lies in using an epitranscriptomic inhibitor not to kill cells, but to reprogram a dysfunctional tumor suppressor pathway back to its physiological, disease-suppressing state in non-cancerous tissues.

ideas.title: Adapting Antibiotic Spectrum Targeting for Selective tRNA Demethylation
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that global reduction of tRNA m5C is the desired outcome by proposing a precision-targeting approach, analogous to the development of narrow-spectrum antibiotics in clinical microbiology. In that field, the destructive broad-spectrum approach was refined by creating agents that selectively inhibit pathogenic bacteria based on unique molecular targets, sparing the beneficial microbiome. Adapted to tRNA biology, the research would focus on developing 'precision demethylation' tools—such as context-dependent enzyme inhibitors or tRNA-isoacceptor-specific antisense oligonucleotides—that disrupt m5C deposition only on specific tRNA subsets implicated in a disease process (e.g., those decoding oncogenic codon biases), thereby aiming to achieve therapeutic benefits while minimizing off-target translational disruption and maintaining homeostasis in healthy cells.

ideas.title: Apply phage display peptide library screening to NSUNs
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from antibody and peptide drug discovery—phage display technology—to the challenge of finding selective binders for NSUN2. In the analogous field, phage display libraries, where each phage particle physically links a displayed peptide to its encoding DNA, are screened against purified protein targets to discover high-affinity binders. The adapted research idea involves screening a diverse phage-displayed peptide library against purified NSUN2 protein, isolating bound phages, and sequencing their DNA to identify peptide sequences that selectively bind NSUN2. This provides an alternative, biologically encoded library approach to DNA-encoded chemical libraries for discovering potential inhibitors or modulators of this RNA methyltransferase.

ideas.title: Integrated Co-Design of Systemic PK and Local Tumor Microenvironment Penetration for Proximity-Inducing Molecules
ideas.description: This project creates a unified design platform that concurrently optimizes the systemic drug-like properties and the local tumor penetration of proximity-inducing small molecules. It employs integrated computational models that simulate both whole-body pharmacokinetics and diffusion through the complex tumor stroma to predict and rank candidate molecules. The top designs are synthesized and experimentally profiled using advanced 3D tumor models that quantify intracellular concentration and target engagement as a function of depth into the tissue. This co-design approach ensures molecules are intrinsically engineered for effective delivery to their site of action within solid tumors, overcoming the traditional trade-off between circulation stability and tissue penetration.

ideas.title: Deciphering the Primary Causal Cascade Following Acute tRNA m5C Depletion Using a Rapidly Inducible Degradation System
ideas.description: This study implements a chemical-genetic degron system to achieve acute and reversible depletion of NSUN2, enabling the dissection of the direct consequences of tRNA m5C loss from secondary adaptations. By triggering synchronous NSUN2 degradation and performing high-temporal-resolution ribosome profiling, tRNA sequencing, and metabolomics within the first few hours, the system captures the initial translational and metabolic perturbations. This approach identifies the earliest stalled ribosome signatures, specific tRNA fragment generation, and metabolic bottlenecks directly caused by the modification loss. The reversibility of the system confirms the causality of observed effects, providing a clear molecular roadmap linking tRNA m5C loss to downstream cellular phenotypes.

ideas.title: Validate Therapeutic Window Using Pharmacokinetic-Pharmacodynamic Modeling Adapted from Antibiotic Development
ideas.description: This research idea adapts the solution from infectious disease research, where pharmacokinetic-pharmacodynamic (PK/PD) modeling is standard for defining the therapeutic window of antibiotics in animal infection models. The analogous solution involves measuring drug exposure, microbiological kill curves, and host toxicity to build predictive models for human dosing. Adapted to this context, the idea is to rigorously assess the efficacy and safety of the proximity-inducing molecule in animal models of TP53 mutant cancer by employing a PK/PD framework. This entails serial measurements of the molecule's plasma and tumor pharmacokinetics, dynamic pharmacodynamic biomarkers of target engagement (e.g., mutant p53 protein levels, pathway modulation), antitumor efficacy (tumor growth inhibition), and comprehensive safety endpoints. The resulting integrated PK/PD-toxicity model will quantitatively define the exposure-response relationships for both benefit and risk, providing a robust, predictive definition of the therapeutic window to inform clinical trial design.

ideas.title: Apply Supramolecular 'Stealth' and 'Addressing' Strategies to Proximity-Inducing Molecules
ideas.description: This idea adapts a core solution from nanocarrier drug delivery and supramolecular chemistry to improve the drug-like properties and tumor penetration of proximity-inducing small molecules. The analogous field solves the problem of delivering unstable or poorly targeted payloads by encapsulating them in a protective, functionalizable shell that provides stealth (improved stability and pharmacokinetics) and addressing (targeted delivery). The adapted research idea involves designing the proximity-inducing molecule as a modular core within a programmable, self-assembling supramolecular complex. The complex's exterior 'cloak' would be rationally designed from biocompatible building blocks to confer optimal ADME properties and incorporate tumor-specific penetration or activation mechanisms, thereby enhancing the molecule's stability in circulation and its efficient, localized action within the tumor microenvironment.

ideas.title: Context-Dependent Selectivity Profiling of NSUN2 Inhibitors via a Multi-Omic Phenotypic Screen
ideas.description: This idea adapts the multi-parametric, panel-based screening strategy common in oncology drug discovery to systematically profile NSUN2 inhibitor selectivity. Instead of testing in a few cell lines, a diverse panel of cellular contexts (various cancer cell lines, non-transformed lines, and relevant primary cells) will be treated with NSUN2 inhibitors. The key adaptation is employing a multi-omic phenotypic screen: downstream effects will be measured not by a single endpoint but via integrated molecular readouts, including direct assessment of RNA methylation changes (m5C mapping), proteomic shifts, and metabolomic profiles. By correlating these inhibitor-induced molecular phenotypes with each cell line's baseline molecular characteristics (e.g., NSUN2 expression, interacting partner levels, metabolic state), the study will map the contextual determinants of inhibitor selectivity, efficacy, and off-target effects, moving beyond a binary selectivity assessment to a functional, systems-level profile.

ideas.title: Comparative Mechanistic Dissection of NSUN2 Selectivity: Covalent vs. Reversible Inhibition
ideas.description: This study directly tests the hypothesis that covalent inhibition is necessary for NSUN2 selectivity by conducting an unbiased, parallel search for both reversible and covalent inhibitors. Starting from a diverse screening library, the best hits from each class will be optimized through independent medicinal chemistry campaigns. The selectivity profiles, binding kinetics, and structural modes of action for the top reversible and covalent compounds will be compared using a suite of biochemical, biophysical, and cellular assays. This approach will definitively reveal whether high selectivity is intrinsically linked to covalent engagement or can be achieved through alternative mechanisms.

ideas.title: Synergistic Combination Therapy Using Proximity-Induced Sequential Activation
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from Integrated Pest Management in agriculture, which coordinates multiple control methods in a sequence to overcome resistance and enhance efficacy, this research idea adapts the concept of sequential synergy for cancer therapy. The analogous solution involves using one agent to create a specific, favorable condition for the next. Adapted here, the proximity-inducing molecule would be used first not just to bring proteins together, but to force interactions that synthetically rewire a tumor cell's state—for example, forcing a dimerization that transiently inhibits a key survival pathway. This induced 'Achilles heel' is then immediately exploited by a follow-up dose of a precisely matched chemotherapy or targeted drug. This sequential, condition-creating combination strategy aims to achieve supra-additive tumor killing while potentially sparing normal tissues that lack the initial proximity-induced vulnerability state.

ideas.title: Therapeutic Index-Guided Screening of Proximity-Inducer Combinations Using Patient-Derived Tumor and Healthy Tissue Organoids
ideas.description: This redesigned approach systematically addresses the critical risk of synergistic toxicity in combination therapies. It utilizes biobanks of patient-derived tumor organoids (PDTOs) and matched healthy tissue organoids to screen the proximity-inducer molecule against a library of standard agents. A multi-parameter readout—assessing tumor cell kill versus healthy cell viability—generates a therapeutic index for each combination. Only candidates demonstrating a superior index over monotherapeutics, with no predicted severe on-target healthy tissue damage, are advanced into mechanistic studies and subsequent in vivo models, ensuring a robust safety filter is applied prior to clinical consideration.

ideas.title: Apply Prodrug Strategies from Antibiotic Development to Optimize Azetidine Acrylamide NSUN2 Inhibitors
ideas.description: The challenge of optimizing the pharmacokinetic properties of the discovered NSUN2 covalent inhibitors parallels the historical problem of poor oral bioavailability in early antibiotic classes like penicillins. That field successfully employed prodrug strategies, specifically esterification, to mask polar groups and enhance absorption. This idea adapts that solution by proposing the design, synthesis, and evaluation of ester prodrug derivatives of the azetidine acrylamide core. The goal is to improve key parameters such as oral bioavailability and plasma half-life, thereby enabling robust in vivo efficacy studies for target validation and therapeutic development.
